name: Cháo Gà
description: Vietnamese rice porridge cooked low and slow until silky and thick, with poached chicken pulled apart by hand, crispy fried shallots, shredded ginger, and a tangle of fresh herbs. Eaten for breakfast, for illness, and for any morning when you need to be held.
tagline: Rice porridge for slow mornings and sore throats
ingredients:
- 200g jasmine rice
- 2L water or light chicken stock
- 2 chicken thighs, bone-in
- 3cm knob of ginger, bruised
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt
- White pepper
- To serve: fried shallots, sliced scallions, fresh ginger julienned, cilantro, lime wedges
instructions:
1. Bring water and ginger to a boil. Add chicken thighs and poach on low heat for 20–25 minutes until cooked through. Remove chicken and shred by hand when cool.
2. Add rinsed rice to the poaching liquid. Cook on medium-low, stirring occasionally, for 30 minutes until the porridge is thick and creamy.
3. Season with fish sauce, salt, and white pepper. Add more water if it thickens too much.
4. Serve in bowls, topped with shredded chicken, fried shallots, scallions, ginger, and cilantro. Squeeze lime over.
tips: The longer you cook it, the more the rice breaks down into a silky, almost seamless texture. Don't rush it.

name: Xôi Gà
description: Glutinous rice steamed until sticky and fragrant, topped with hand-shredded poached chicken, crispy fried shallots, and a drizzle of ginger scallion oil. Sold from push carts and eaten standing up — or slow Sunday mornings at home.
tagline: Sticky rice crowned with shredded chicken
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 1 whole chicken leg (or 2 thighs)
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt
- ½ tsp turmeric powder
- For ginger-scallion oil: 3 scallion stalks, sliced + 1 tbsp fresh ginger, minced + 3 tbsp neutral oil, heated until smoking
- Fried shallots
- Maggi seasoning sauce (optional)
instructions:
1. Drain soaked glutinous rice. Toss with salt and turmeric.
2. Steam rice over boiling water for 35–40 minutes, fluffing halfway through, until fully tender and translucent.
3. Poach chicken leg in salted water with a knob of ginger for 20–25 minutes. Rest, then shred by hand.
4. Make ginger-scallion oil: combine scallion and ginger in a bowl, heat oil until smoking, pour over. It will sizzle.
5. Season shredded chicken with fish sauce and a spoonful of the ginger-scallion oil.
6. Serve rice mounded, topped with chicken, fried shallots, and ginger-scallion oil.
tips: Don't skip soaking the rice overnight — unsoaked glutinous rice steams unevenly. The hot oil poured over raw scallion is the technique that makes this.

name: Bánh Mì Ốp La
description: The simplest bánh mì — a crispy baguette split open and loaded with a fried egg (sunny-side up, slightly crispy edges), pâté, butter, Maggi sauce, and a fistful of cilantro. Street food that exists in that perfect breakfast-or-anytime zone.
tagline: Egg and pâté on bread — Việt Nam's breakfast icon
ingredients:
- 1 Vietnamese baguette (or demi-baguette), halved lengthwise
- 2 eggs
- 1 tbsp butter
- 2 tbsp chicken liver pâté
- Maggi seasoning sauce
- Fresh cilantro, whole leaves
- Sliced chili
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Toast or warm the baguette in a dry pan or oven until the crust is crispy.
2. Melt butter in a pan over medium-high heat until foamy. Crack in the eggs.
3. Fry until the whites are set and edges are just crispy, yolk still runny. Season with salt and pepper.
4. Spread pâté on both inner sides of the baguette. Lay in the eggs.
5. Add a few drops of Maggi, pile on cilantro and sliced chili. Close and eat immediately.
tips: The crispy egg edges and runny yolk are non-negotiable. High heat, butter, and don't touch it for the first 90 seconds.

name: Phở Bò
description: The great Northern beef noodle soup — a bone broth simmered for hours with charred ginger, star anise, cloves, and cinnamon until it turns amber, sweet, and impossibly clear. Poured over thin rice noodles and paper-thin slices of raw beef that cook in the bowl. Eaten with bean sprouts, fresh herbs, hoisin, and chili.
tagline: The soul of Hà Nội in a bowl
ingredients:
- 1.5kg beef marrow bones and knuckle bones
- 500g beef brisket or chuck
- 1 large onion, halved
- 8cm knob of ginger, halved
- 3 star anise
- 3 whole cloves
- 1 cinnamon stick
- 1 black cardamom pod
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, plus more to taste
- 1 tsp sugar
- 500g flat rice noodles (bánh phở), cooked
- 200g beef sirloin or eye of round, sliced paper-thin (partially freeze for easier slicing)
- Bean sprouts, Thai basil, sliced chili, lime, hoisin sauce, sriracha to serve
instructions:
1. Blanch bones in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain and rinse thoroughly under cold water — this removes impurities and keeps the broth clear.
2. Char ginger and onion directly over a gas flame or under a broiler until blackened in spots. This adds smokiness and depth.
3. Toast spices in a dry pan until fragrant, 1–2 minutes.
4. In a large pot, cover bones with 4 liters of water. Bring to a boil, skim foam, then reduce to a bare simmer.
5. Add charred ginger, onion, toasted spices, and brisket. Simmer very gently for at least 3 hours, skimming occasionally.
6. Remove brisket after 1.5–2 hours when tender. Slice thinly.
7. Strain broth through a fine mesh. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
8. Assemble bowls: cooked noodles, a few slices of brisket, raw beef layered on top. Pour boiling hot broth over — the raw beef will cook in the bowl.
9. Serve with garnishes on the side.
tips: Clear broth comes from two things: a thorough blanch of the bones and keeping the simmer at the barest bubble. Never let it boil.

name: Phở Gà
description: Lighter, cleaner, and more delicate than beef phở — made with a whole chicken, charred ginger and onion, and the same warming spices. The broth turns golden and crystal clear. Served with sliced chicken, rice noodles, and all the usual herbs.
tagline: Lighter than beef, just as comforting
ingredients:
- 1 whole chicken (about 1.5kg)
- 1 large onion, halved and charred
- 6cm knob of ginger, halved and charred
- 2 star anise
- 1 cinnamon stick
- 3 whole cloves
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar
- 400g flat rice noodles, cooked
- Bean sprouts, fresh Thai basil, scallions, sliced chili, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Char onion and ginger directly over flame or under broiler. Toast spices in dry pan until fragrant.
2. Place whole chicken in a large pot, cover with 3 liters cold water. Bring to a boil, skim foam.
3. Add charred aromatics and spices. Reduce to a gentle simmer for 60–70 minutes until chicken is just cooked.
4. Remove chicken carefully. Let cool, then pull meat from bones by hand. Discard skin.
5. Strain broth. Season with fish sauce, salt, and sugar.
6. Return broth to a boil. Assemble bowls with noodles and chicken slices. Pour boiling broth over.
7. Serve with garnishes.
tips: Don't boil the chicken aggressively or the meat becomes dry and the broth turns cloudy. A gentle simmer produces the clearest, most delicate result.

name: Bún Bò Huế
description: The fiercer, more complex cousin of phở from the old imperial capital. A rich pork and beef broth stained red with shrimp paste and lemongrass, with thick round noodles, sliced beef shank, pork knuckle, and — if you're not afraid of it — congealed blood. Deeply savory, deeply spicy, unforgettable.
tagline: Spicy, fragrant, unmistakably Huế
ingredients:
- 500g beef shank
- 500g pork knuckle or pork hock
- 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised and cut in half
- 3 tbsp mắm ruốc (shrimp paste), dissolved in ½ cup hot water and strained
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 2 tsp annatto oil or chili oil (for color)
- 3 dried red chilies or 2 tsp chili flakes
- 2 shallots, charred
- 500g thick round rice noodles (bún bò Huế noodles)
- To serve: shredded banana blossom, bean sprouts, sliced scallions, cilantro, lime, sliced chili
instructions:
1. Blanch beef shank and pork knuckle in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain and rinse.
2. In a large pot, cover meat with 3 liters of water. Bring to boil, skim foam.
3. Add charred shallots and lemongrass. Reduce to a simmer. Cook for 1.5–2 hours until meat is tender.
4. Remove meat. Slice beef shank thinly. Pull pork off the bone.
5. Strain broth. Return to pot.
6. Add strained shrimp paste liquid, fish sauce, sugar, annatto oil, and chilies. Simmer 20 minutes. Adjust seasoning — it should be savory, slightly funky, and richly spiced.
7. Cook noodles according to package. Assemble bowls with noodles and meat. Pour hot broth over.
8. Serve with banana blossom, bean sprouts, herbs, lime.
tips: The mắm ruốc is what makes bún bò Huế taste like itself. Don't skip it — there's no substitute for that deep, fermented funk.

name: Bún Riêu Cua
description: A tomato-red broth enriched with freshwater crab paste — the crab forms soft, fluffy pink-orange curds on the surface as it cooks. Served over thin vermicelli with fried tofu, shrimp paste on the side, and a plate of water spinach and banana blossoms.
tagline: Tangy broth, pillowy crab paste, a bowl of the south
ingredients:
- 200g freshwater crab paste (mắm cua, from a jar or freshly ground)
- 3 eggs, beaten
- 4 ripe tomatoes, cut into wedges
- 200g fried tofu puffs, halved
- 1.5L pork or chicken stock
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar
- 500g thin rice vermicelli, cooked
- To serve: sliced scallions, fried shallots, rau muống (water spinach) or bean sprouts, mắm tôm (shrimp paste), lime, fresh chili
instructions:
1. Mix crab paste with beaten eggs. Season with a pinch of salt.
2. Bring stock to a boil. Add crab-egg mixture and stir gently just once — you want large curds to form on the surface, not scrambled.
3. When curd forms (2–3 minutes), skim carefully with a large spoon and set aside.
4. Add tomatoes and simmer 10 minutes. Add tofu puffs.
5. Season broth with fish sauce, salt, and sugar.
6. Return crab curds to the pot just before serving.
7. Assemble bowls with cooked vermicelli. Ladle broth with curds and tofu over.
8. Top with scallions and fried shallots. Serve with greens, mắm tôm, lime, and chili.
tips: The crab curd is delicate — add it at the end and don't stir. A heavy-handed cook will break the curds and lose the signature texture.

name: Bún Chả
description: Hà Nội's great lunch — charcoal-grilled pork patties and pork belly strips bobbing in a vinegary, sweet nước chấm broth, served alongside cold rice vermicelli, a mountain of fresh herbs (perilla, mint, lettuce), and green papaya pickle. Eaten by dunking the noodles directly into the bowl.
tagline: Grilled pork, cold noodles — Obama's lunch in Hà Nội
ingredients:
- 400g ground pork (for patties)
- 300g pork belly, thinly sliced
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 3 shallots, minced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Black pepper
- For dipping broth: 4 tbsp fish sauce, 3 tbsp sugar, 3 tbsp rice vinegar, 1 cup warm water, 2 cloves garlic sliced, 1 chili sliced
- 400g rice vermicelli, cooked and cooled
- To serve: perilla (tía tô), mint, butter lettuce, green papaya or carrot pickle
instructions:
1. Mix ground pork with half the garlic, half the shallot, fish sauce, sugar, and pepper. Form into small flat patties.
2. Marinate pork belly slices in remaining garlic, shallot, fish sauce, sugar, and pepper for 15 minutes.
3. Make dipping broth: dissolve sugar in warm water, add fish sauce, vinegar, garlic, and chili. Taste — it should be balanced: sweet, sour, savory.
4. Grill pork patties and belly over high heat (charcoal if possible) until charred at the edges. Add to the dipping broth bowl while still hot.
5. Serve with a large plate of cold vermicelli, fresh herbs, and lettuce.
6. To eat: pick up herbs and noodles, dip into the broth bowl.
tips: The trick is eating everything from one communal bowl of broth. The broth gets richer as the grilled pork fat drips into it throughout the meal.

name: Cơm Tấm
description: Broken rice is the soul of Sài Gòn street food. The fractured grains cook up softer and stickier than regular rice, soaking up the juices from the grilled pork chop beside it. Served with a fried egg, bì (shredded pork), mỡ hành (scallion oil), and a bowl of nước chấm.
tagline: Broken rice with grilled pork — the taste of Sài Gòn
ingredients:
- 300g broken jasmine rice (gạo tấm)
- 2 pork chops, bone-in, about 1cm thick
- 3 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 1 tsp dark soy sauce
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 stalk lemongrass, minced
- 2 fried eggs
- For mỡ hành: 3 scallion stalks, sliced + 2 tbsp neutral oil, heated until smoking
- For nước chấm: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, ½ cup warm water, 1 clove garlic, 1 chili
instructions:
1. Marinate pork chops in fish sauce, sugar, soy sauce, garlic, and lemongrass for at least 1 hour.
2. Rinse broken rice and cook with slightly less water than regular rice.
3. Make nước chấm: dissolve sugar in warm water, add fish sauce, lime juice, minced garlic, and sliced chili.
4. Grill or pan-fry pork over high heat, 3–4 minutes per side until charred at the edges. Rest 5 minutes before slicing.
5. Make mỡ hành: heat oil until smoking, pour over sliced scallions.
6. Serve rice mounded, with pork, fried egg, and scallion oil. Nước chấm on the side.
tips: The char on the pork is everything. Cook over high heat and resist moving it so the sugar in the marinade can caramelize.

name: Thịt Kho Tàu
description: Pork belly and hard-boiled eggs braised low and slow in coconut water and caramel until the fat turns glossy, trembling, and impossibly tender. This is Tết food, comfort food, the kind of thing your grandmother keeps on the stove all day. The sauce turns brick-red and tastes like something between a braise and a candy.
tagline: Braised pork belly and eggs — the taste of home
ingredients:
- 700g pork belly, cut into 4cm pieces
- 4 hard-boiled eggs, peeled
- 400ml fresh coconut water
- 3 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 2 tbsp caramel sauce (nước màu): cook 2 tbsp sugar dry until deep amber, add 1 tbsp water carefully
- 3 cloves garlic, smashed
- 3 shallots, halved
- White pepper
- Steamed jasmine rice, to serve
instructions:
1. Make caramel: heat sugar in a dry pot over medium until it melts and turns deep amber. Add 1 tbsp water carefully. Set aside.
2. Blanch pork belly in boiling water for 5 minutes. Drain and pat dry.
3. Combine pork, coconut water, fish sauce, caramel sauce, garlic, and shallots in a heavy pot. Bring to a boil.
4. Skim foam, reduce to a very gentle simmer. Cover and cook 45 minutes.
5. Add peeled eggs, continue cooking uncovered for 25–30 minutes until sauce reduces and clings to the pork.
6. Season with white pepper. Serve with steamed rice and pickled mustard greens.
tips: Coconut water — not coconut milk — is the key. It adds a subtle sweetness without heaviness. The longer it braises, the more the fat renders into something translucent and silky.

name: Cá Kho Tộ
description: Catfish braised in a clay pot with caramel, fish sauce, and cracked black pepper until the sauce reduces to a thick, sticky lacquer. Eaten with plain rice that soaks up every drop. One of the most elemental Vietnamese dishes — five ingredients, unforgettable.
tagline: Catfish caramelized in a clay pot, eaten with plain rice
ingredients:
- 500g catfish steaks (or any firm fish)
- 3 tbsp fish sauce
- 2 tbsp caramel sauce (nước màu): 2 tbsp sugar cooked until deep amber + 1 tbsp water
- 3 shallots, sliced
- 3 cloves garlic, sliced
- 1 tsp freshly cracked black pepper
- ½ cup water
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Scallions and chili to finish
instructions:
1. Make caramel: cook sugar in a small pot until deep mahogany. Add 1 tbsp water carefully. Set aside.
2. Season fish with a little fish sauce and pepper. Let sit 10 minutes.
3. Heat oil in a clay pot or heavy pan. Fry shallots and garlic until golden and fragrant.
4. Add fish in a single layer. Brown lightly on each side.
5. Add caramel sauce, remaining fish sauce, and water. Bring to a boil.
6. Reduce to very low heat, cover, and braise 25 minutes.
7. Uncover and continue cooking, basting occasionally, until sauce is thick and lacquered, 15 more minutes.
8. Garnish with scallions and chili. Serve over steamed rice.
tips: Cook the caramel until it's a deep mahogany — the slight bitterness it develops is what balances the fish sauce's saltiness.

name: Bò Kho
description: A Vietnamese beef stew that manages to be both French-influenced and entirely its own — carrots, star anise, lemongrass, and fish sauce in a deep red broth. Eaten with a crusty baguette torn and dunked in, or poured over thick rice noodles.
tagline: Slow-braised beef stew — a Sunday morning classic
ingredients:
- 800g beef chuck or brisket, cut into 4cm cubes
- 3 large carrots, cut into chunks
- 2 stalks lemongrass, bruised and halved
- 3 star anise
- 1 cinnamon stick
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 3 shallots, sliced
- 2 tbsp tomato paste
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp annatto powder or paprika (for color)
- 1 tsp sugar
- 500ml beef stock or water
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Vietnamese or French baguette, to serve
instructions:
1. Pat beef dry. Brown in batches over high heat in oil until deep crust forms. Set aside.
2. In the same pot, soften shallots and garlic. Add tomato paste, cook 2 minutes.
3. Add annatto, spices, and lemongrass. Stir to coat.
4. Return beef to pot. Add fish sauce, sugar, and stock. Bring to a boil.
5. Skim foam, reduce to a gentle simmer. Cover and cook 1.5 hours.
6. Add carrots in the last 30 minutes.
7. Taste and adjust seasoning. The broth should be rich and slightly sweet.
8. Serve in bowls with torn baguette on the side.
tips: Brown the beef properly — this step builds the flavor base. Don't crowd the pan, work in batches.

name: Bún Thịt Nướng
description: A cold vermicelli bowl topped with caramelized grilled pork, fresh herbs, cucumber, pickled vegetables, and a pool of nước chấm that ties everything together. One of those dishes that's complex in flavor but forgiving to make.
tagline: Fire-kissed pork over cold vermicelli
ingredients:
- 400g pork shoulder or pork belly, thinly sliced
- 3 tbsp fish sauce
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 2 stalks lemongrass, minced
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 300g rice vermicelli, cooked and cooled
- For bowls: shredded lettuce, bean sprouts, sliced cucumber, pickled daikon and carrot, fresh mint, cilantro
- Crushed roasted peanuts
- For nước chấm: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, ½ cup water, garlic, chili
instructions:
1. Combine fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass, garlic, shallots, and oil. Marinate pork for at least 30 minutes.
2. Make nước chấm: dissolve sugar in water, add fish sauce, lime juice, minced garlic, and chili.
3. Grill pork over high heat until charred and caramelized, about 3–4 minutes per side. Let rest, then slice.
4. Build bowls: noodles on the bottom, then lettuce, bean sprouts, cucumber, herbs, and pickles.
5. Lay grilled pork over the top.
6. Pour nước chấm over everything. Scatter peanuts on top.
tips: The pork should be thin enough to char quickly without drying out. Partially freeze it for 20 minutes before slicing for clean, even cuts.

name: Chả Cá Lã Vọng
description: Hà Nội's most famous dish — catfish marinated in turmeric and galangal, pan-fried until golden, then finished tableside in a sizzling pan with a mountain of fresh dill and scallions. Eaten over vermicelli with roasted peanuts and a side of mắm tôm.
tagline: Turmeric fish, dill, and bún — a Hà Nội original
ingredients:
- 600g catfish fillets, cut into 4cm pieces
- 1 tbsp turmeric powder
- 2 tbsp galangal paste or freshly grated galangal
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 3 large bunches fresh dill, roughly chopped
- 6 scallion stalks, cut into 4cm lengths
- 300g rice vermicelli, cooked
- Roasted peanuts, crushed
- For mắm tôm sauce: 2 tbsp shrimp paste, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp water — whisk until dissolved
instructions:
1. Marinate fish in turmeric, galangal, fish sauce, and oil for at least 1 hour.
2. Heat a large pan over medium-high. Pan-fry fish pieces until golden on both sides, about 3–4 minutes per side. Don't crowd the pan.
3. Pile dill and scallions over the fish in the pan. Toss briefly — the dill should wilt but still be vibrant.
4. To serve: place noodles in bowls. Spoon fish and dill directly from pan.
5. Scatter peanuts on top. Serve with mắm tôm sauce on the side.
tips: The turmeric will stain everything. Wear an apron and use a pan you don't mind discoloring. The generous dill is not optional — it IS the dish.

name: Bò Lúc Lắc
description: Cubes of beef sirloin wok-tossed over blistering heat until a deep mahogany crust forms on every side. Served over fresh watercress with a lime-black pepper dipping salt and sliced tomatoes. Fast, intensely savory, and best eaten immediately.
tagline: Shaking beef, wok-charred and tender
ingredients:
- 400g beef sirloin or tenderloin, cut into 3cm cubes
- 2 tbsp oyster sauce
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp neutral oil with high smoke point
- Watercress or butter lettuce
- Sliced tomatoes and red onion
- For dipping salt: 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp black pepper, juice of 1 lime — mix in a small bowl
instructions:
1. Marinate beef cubes in oyster sauce, fish sauce, soy sauce, sugar, and garlic for 15 minutes.
2. Make dipping salt.
3. Arrange watercress on a platter with sliced tomatoes and onion.
4. Heat a wok or heavy pan until smoking. Add oil.
5. Add beef in a single layer. Cook without touching for 1 minute to form a crust.
6. Toss and 'shake' the pan vigorously, cooking 2–3 more minutes until the crust is on all sides and beef is medium-rare inside.
7. Pour over the watercress immediately. Serve with dipping salt.
tips: Use the hottest heat you have. The crust forms from the high sugar and soy caramelizing — it only happens at high temperature, not medium.

name: Bánh Xèo
description: A crêpe so thin and crispy it sizzles when the batter hits the pan — hence the name, which means 'sizzling cake.' Turmeric-yellow, filled with pork, shrimp, and bean sprouts, eaten torn and wrapped in mustard leaf with herbs and dipped in nước chấm. The mess is part of the experience.
tagline: Sizzling crêpe, crispy edges, eaten rolled in lettuce
ingredients:
- For batter: 200g rice flour, ½ tsp turmeric powder, ¼ tsp salt, 350ml coconut milk, 100ml water, 2 scallions sliced
- 200g pork shoulder, thinly sliced
- 200g medium shrimp, peeled
- 200g bean sprouts
- 1 onion, thinly sliced
- Neutral oil for frying
- To wrap and serve: mustard leaf, butter lettuce, fresh mint, perilla, cucumber, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Whisk batter ingredients until smooth. Rest 30 minutes.
2. Season pork and shrimp with a pinch of salt and pepper.
3. Heat a non-stick pan over high heat. Add 1 tbsp oil.
4. Cook a few pieces of shrimp and pork for 1 minute.
5. Add a few slices of onion, then pour in about 80ml batter, swirling to coat the pan in a thin layer.
6. Scatter bean sprouts over one half. Cover and cook 2 minutes.
7. Uncover, cook until edges are deeply crispy and golden, 2–3 more minutes.
8. Fold in half and slide onto a plate. Repeat.
9. Eat immediately, torn and wrapped in greens, with nước chấm.
tips: The pan must be very hot and the batter very thin. Too thick and it steams instead of sizzles — you'll lose the crispy edges.

name: Gà Nướng Sả
description: Chicken thighs marinated in lemongrass, fish sauce, garlic, and a little sugar, then grilled or roasted until the skin is caramelized and fragrant. The marinade chars at the edges, creating bittersweet pockets of flavor.
tagline: Lemongrass chicken, grilled over charcoal
ingredients:
- 6 bone-in chicken thighs, skin-on
- 3 stalks lemongrass, white parts only, minced very fine
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 1 tsp turmeric powder
- Black pepper
- Lime wedges and steamed rice to serve
instructions:
1. Score chicken thighs through the skin to the bone.
2. Mix lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, oil, turmeric, and pepper into a paste. Rub all over chicken, getting under the skin.
3. Marinate at least 1 hour, ideally overnight.
4. To grill: cook over medium-high heat, skin-side down first, 6–8 minutes per side until cooked through and charred.
5. To roast: 220°C for 30–35 minutes, skin side up, until the skin is deeply golden.
6. Rest 5 minutes before serving with rice and lime.
tips: Mince the lemongrass as fine as possible — you want it to become a paste that adheres to the skin and caramelizes, not remain as fibrous chunks.

name: Tôm Rim
description: Shrimp cooked in a sticky, lacquered caramel sauce with garlic, shallots, and fish sauce until each piece is glossy and candied at the edges. Eaten over rice. On the table in under 20 minutes.
tagline: Caramelized shrimp, sticky and full of umami
ingredients:
- 400g large shrimp, shell-on (or peeled)
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- Black pepper
- Sliced scallions to serve
instructions:
1. Cook sugar in a dry pan over medium heat until it melts and turns amber caramel.
2. Add oil, garlic, and shallots — it will spit. Stir 30 seconds.
3. Add shrimp. Toss to coat in caramel.
4. Add fish sauce and a splash of water.
5. Cook on medium-high, tossing regularly, until shrimp are cooked through and sauce is thick and clings to every piece, about 6–8 minutes.
6. Season with black pepper. Serve over rice, topped with scallions.
tips: Keep the shell on for more flavor and a crunchier caramelized coating. The shells are delicious and edible.

name: Gà Kho Gừng
description: Chicken braised in caramel and fish sauce with an assertive amount of fresh ginger — the ginger slices soften and sweeten as they cook, becoming almost candied. The sauce reduces to a sticky glaze. A weeknight dish that tastes like it took much longer.
tagline: Ginger chicken — quiet heat, gentle warmth
ingredients:
- 800g chicken thighs, bone-in
- 5cm fresh ginger, peeled and cut into thin matchsticks
- 3 shallots, sliced
- 2 cloves garlic, smashed
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp caramel sauce (nước màu)
- ½ cup water
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- White pepper and scallions to serve
instructions:
1. Pat chicken dry. Brown in oil over medium-high heat, skin-side down, until golden. Remove.
2. In the same pan, fry shallots and garlic until fragrant.
3. Return chicken. Add caramel sauce, fish sauce, sugar, ginger, and water.
4. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer. Cover and cook 20 minutes.
5. Uncover, increase heat, and cook 10–15 more minutes until sauce has reduced and coats the chicken.
6. Finish with white pepper and scallions.
tips: Don't skimp on the ginger. The quantity seems like a lot but it mellows significantly during braising.

name: Canh Chua
description: A tamarind-sour broth with fish, pineapple, tomato, bean sprouts, and okra — Vietnamese home cooking at its most honest. Sour, sweet, savory, and fragrant with rice paddy herb. Made in under 30 minutes. Best eaten alongside a plate of rice and cá kho.
tagline: Sweet and sour soup that tastes like Miền Nam
ingredients:
- 400g catfish or tilapia, cut into steaks
- 2 tbsp tamarind paste dissolved in ½ cup hot water, strained
- 1L water or light stock
- 150g pineapple, cubed
- 2 tomatoes, quartered
- 100g okra, halved
- 100g bean sprouts
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar
- Rice paddy herb (ngò ôm) or dill, to finish
- Fried shallots
instructions:
1. Bring water to a boil. Add tamarind liquid, fish sauce, and sugar. Taste — it should be pleasantly sour.
2. Add pineapple and tomatoes. Simmer 5 minutes.
3. Add fish and okra. Cook gently 8–10 minutes until fish is just cooked.
4. Add bean sprouts in the last minute.
5. Turn off heat. Add rice paddy herb.
6. Serve topped with fried shallots, alongside rice.
tips: Add the fish gently and don't stir — the pieces are delicate and will fall apart. The sourness should be front and center.

name: Canh Khổ Qua Nhồi Thịt
description: Bitter melon halved and hollowed, stuffed with a pork and mushroom filling, and simmered in a clear pork stock until the melon turns translucent and its bitterness mellows into something almost gentle. A Vietnamese grandmother soup — patient and restorative.
tagline: Bitter melon stuffed with pork — bittersweet on purpose
ingredients:
- 2 bitter melons (khổ qua), halved lengthwise, seeds scooped out
- 300g ground pork
- 30g glass noodles, soaked and roughly chopped
- 20g dried wood ear mushrooms, soaked and minced
- 1 shallot, minced
- 1 egg
- 1 tbsp fish sauce, salt and pepper
- 1.2L pork or chicken stock
- Scallions and cilantro to serve
instructions:
1. Mix ground pork, glass noodles, mushrooms, shallot, egg, fish sauce, salt, and pepper until combined.
2. Pack filling tightly into the hollowed bitter melon halves.
3. Bring stock to a boil. Gently lower stuffed melons in.
4. Simmer on low heat for 30–35 minutes until melon is tender and filling is cooked through.
5. Taste broth, adjust seasoning.
6. Serve in a bowl, cut into sections, topped with scallions and cilantro.
tips: The bitterness is the point — it's considered cooling and healthy in Vietnamese cooking. Don't salt the melon first or it loses its character.

name: Đồ Chua
description: The quick-pickled carrot and daikon that lives inside every bánh mì and alongside every Vietnamese grilled plate. Vinegar, sugar, salt, and thirty minutes of patience. They last a week in the fridge and improve everything they touch.
tagline: Sweet and sour pickled carrot and daikon
ingredients:
- 1 large carrot, julienned into thin matchsticks
- 200g daikon, julienned into thin matchsticks
- 1 tsp salt
- 1 tsp sugar (for salting step)
- For pickling brine: ½ cup white vinegar or rice vinegar, ½ cup warm water, 2 tbsp sugar, 1 tsp salt
instructions:
1. Toss carrot and daikon with salt and 1 tsp sugar. Let sit 10 minutes until slightly wilted.
2. Squeeze firmly with your hands to remove excess moisture. Rinse under cold water and squeeze dry again.
3. Make brine: dissolve sugar and salt in warm water, add vinegar.
4. Pack vegetables into a clean jar. Pour brine over until submerged.
5. Let sit at room temperature for at least 30 minutes before using.
6. Refrigerate for up to 1 week.
tips: The wilting and squeezing step is essential — it removes water so the vegetables stay crisp and the brine doesn't get diluted.

name: Mỡ Hành
description: The simplest Vietnamese condiment — sliced scallions scalded with smoking hot oil until they soften and release their sweetness and fragrance. Drizzled over everything: cơm tấm, xôi, cháo, and especially plain steamed rice when there's nothing else.
tagline: Scallion oil — poured hot over everything
ingredients:
- 4 stalks scallions, thinly sliced
- 3 tbsp neutral oil (vegetable, grapeseed, or canola)
- Pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Place scallions in a small heatproof bowl. Add a pinch of salt.
2. Heat oil in a small pan over high heat until it just begins to smoke — you'll see ripples forming across the surface.
3. Immediately pour hot oil over scallions. It will sizzle and pop.
4. Stir briefly. Use immediately or store at room temperature for a few hours.
tips: The oil must be smoking. Too cool and the scallions just sit in oil. Too hot and they burn. The moment before smoke is the window.

name: Gỏi Cuốn
description: Fresh spring rolls that taste like a garden. Translucent rice paper wrapped around shrimp, pork, vermicelli, and a tumble of fresh herbs — light, cooling, and beautiful. Served with peanut dipping sauce.
tagline: Fresh herbs, shrimp, pork, rolled tight in rice paper
ingredients:
- 12 round rice paper wrappers (bánh tráng)
- 200g medium shrimp, cooked and halved lengthwise
- 150g pork belly, boiled and sliced thin
- 100g thin vermicelli noodles, cooked
- Butter lettuce, fresh mint, perilla (tía tô), sliced cucumber
- For peanut sauce: 3 tbsp hoisin sauce, 2 tbsp peanut butter, 1 tbsp lime juice, warm water to thin, crushed peanuts, sliced chili
instructions:
1. Prepare all fillings before rolling — have everything ready and at hand.
2. Make peanut sauce: whisk hoisin, peanut butter, lime juice with warm water to a dippable consistency.
3. Fill a wide shallow bowl with warm water. Dip a rice paper wrapper for 5–8 seconds until just pliable.
4. Lay flat on a board. Place lettuce, herbs, noodles, and pork in the center. Lay shrimp along the bottom third.
5. Fold in sides, then roll tightly upward so the shrimp face outward through the wrapper.
6. Serve with peanut sauce. Eat within 30 minutes.
tips: Don't over-soak the rice paper — it continues softening after you remove it from the water. A few seconds is enough.

name: Chả Giò
description: Crispy fried spring rolls with a filling of pork, glass noodles, wood ear mushrooms, and carrot, wrapped in rice paper and fried until completely shatteringly crisp. The opposite of gỏi cuốn — same vessel, opposite energy.
tagline: Fried spring rolls, gold and crackling
ingredients:
- 300g ground pork
- 50g glass noodles, soaked 10 minutes and roughly chopped
- 30g dried wood ear mushrooms, soaked and minced
- 1 carrot, grated
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 egg
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, salt and pepper
- 15–18 small rice paper wrappers
- Neutral oil for deep frying
- To serve: lettuce, fresh herbs, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Mix pork, glass noodles, mushrooms, carrot, shallots, egg, fish sauce, salt, and pepper until combined.
2. Soften rice paper wrappers briefly in warm water — just until pliable, about 3 seconds.
3. Place 1–2 tbsp filling on the lower third. Fold in sides, roll tightly.
4. Heat oil to 165°C. Fry rolls in batches, not crowding, for 8–10 minutes, turning, until deep golden brown and crispy.
5. Drain on paper towels.
6. Serve wrapped in lettuce with herbs and nước chấm for dipping.
tips: Fry twice for extra crunch: first at 150°C until cooked through, rest, then refry at 180°C until deeply golden just before serving.

name: Bánh Mì
description: The Vietnamese sandwich that belongs to no one and everywhere at once — a French baguette with Vietnamese insides: pâté, butter, thịt nguội (cold cuts), chả lụa (pork roll), đồ chua, fresh cucumber, cilantro, and chili. The bread must be right: thin, shattering crust, airy inside.
tagline: Crusty baguette, pickled veg, layers of flavor
ingredients:
- 2 Vietnamese baguettes (or demi-baguettes)
- 4 tbsp chicken liver pâté
- 2 tbsp unsalted butter, softened
- 100g chả lụa (Vietnamese pork roll), sliced
- 100g thịt nguội (Vietnamese cold cuts) or ham
- Đồ chua (pickled carrots and daikon)
- ½ cucumber, julienned
- Fresh cilantro, whole sprigs
- 1 chili, sliced
- Maggi seasoning sauce or light soy sauce
instructions:
1. Split baguettes lengthwise without cutting all the way through.
2. Spread pâté generously on one side, butter on the other.
3. Layer in chả lụa, cold cuts, a good amount of đồ chua, and cucumber.
4. Add cilantro sprigs and chili.
5. Drizzle a few drops of Maggi over everything.
6. Close firmly. Eat immediately.
tips: The bread is non-negotiable. A proper Vietnamese baguette is lighter and crispier than French baguette. If unavailable, toast a standard baguette at 200°C for 5 minutes to crisp the crust.

name: Nem Lụi
description: Ground pork seasoned with lemongrass and shrimp paste, shaped around skewers of lemongrass, and grilled until charred. A Central Vietnamese specialty eaten wrapped in rice paper with herbs and dipped in mắm nêm — the pungent, funky fermented anchovy sauce that defines Central food.
tagline: Lemongrass skewers, grilled at the table
ingredients:
- 400g ground pork
- 3 stalks lemongrass, minced fine (keep the outer stalks whole for skewers)
- 1 tbsp mắm ruốc (fermented shrimp paste)
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tsp sugar
- Salt and pepper
- 6–8 lemongrass stalks (thick outer stalks), for skewers
- For mắm nêm dipping sauce: 3 tbsp mắm nêm (fermented anchovy sauce), 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp lime juice, pineapple juice, minced garlic and chili
- To serve: rice paper wrappers, lettuce, mint, perilla, cucumber, star fruit
instructions:
1. Mix pork with minced lemongrass, shrimp paste, garlic, sugar, salt, and pepper until very well combined.
2. Shape pork around the thick end of each lemongrass stalk, squeezing and pressing to adhere.
3. Make mắm nêm sauce: whisk all ingredients, adjusting sugar and lime to taste — it should be pungent, sweet, and sour.
4. Grill over high heat, turning, until cooked through and charred, about 12–15 minutes total.
5. Serve with the dipping sauce, rice paper, and all the herbs and vegetables.
tips: The mắm nêm is polarizing but essential for this dish. If it's your first time, start with a small amount on the side.

name: Bánh Cuốn
description: Translucent sheets of rice flour batter steamed on stretched cloth, filled with minced pork and wood ear mushrooms, then rolled and cut into pieces. Silky, delicate, and textural in the best way. Served with crispy fried shallots, sliced chả lụa, and nước chấm.
tagline: Rice rolls steamed to silk, dipped in fish sauce
ingredients:
- For batter: 200g rice flour, 2 tbsp tapioca starch, ½ tsp salt, 600ml water — mix until smooth
- 250g ground pork
- 25g dried wood ear mushrooms, soaked and finely chopped
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- Salt and pepper
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- To serve: chả lụa (pork roll) sliced, fried shallots, fresh cilantro or mint, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Cook filling: stir-fry shallots in oil, add pork and mushrooms. Season with fish sauce, salt, and pepper. Cook until dry. Let cool.
2. Set up a steamer with a tightly stretched cloth over the opening. Bring to a boil.
3. Ladle a thin layer of batter onto the cloth. Cover and steam for 1–2 minutes until the sheet is just set and translucent.
4. Spoon a line of filling across the center. Using a flat spatula, fold and roll the sheet off the cloth.
5. Cut into pieces. Repeat with remaining batter.
6. Serve with fried shallots, chả lụa, and nước chấm.
tips: The batter needs to be thin enough to pour in a very thin layer. If the sheets tear, add a little more water. The cloth must stay taut.

name: Gỏi Gà
description: Shredded poached chicken tossed with shredded cabbage, carrot, fried shallots, rau răm (Vietnamese coriander), and a sharp lime-fish sauce dressing. Light enough to eat a full bowl. Good enough to eat another.
tagline: Shredded chicken salad with mint and lime
ingredients:
- 2 chicken thighs or ½ poached chicken breast
- 200g white cabbage, finely shredded
- 1 carrot, julienned
- 1 small red onion, thinly sliced (soaked in cold water 10 minutes to mellow)
- Large handful rau răm (Vietnamese coriander) or mint
- For dressing: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 clove garlic minced, 1 chili sliced
- Fried shallots
- Crushed roasted peanuts
instructions:
1. Poach chicken in salted water with ginger until cooked, about 20 minutes. Let cool, shred by hand.
2. Make dressing: dissolve sugar in lime juice, add fish sauce, garlic, chili.
3. Toss cabbage, carrot, and red onion in a large bowl. Add dressing and mix well.
4. Add shredded chicken and rau răm. Toss again.
5. Taste and adjust — it should be sharp, savory, and herby.
6. Pile on a plate. Top with fried shallots and peanuts.
tips: Shred the chicken while it's still warm — it pulls apart more cleanly. Dress just before serving so the cabbage stays crisp.

name: Gỏi Đu Đủ
description: Shredded unripe green papaya tossed with dried shrimp, crushed peanuts, fresh herbs, and a sharp lime-fish sauce dressing. Crunchy, sour, salty, and herby. A salad that proves texture is as important as flavor.
tagline: Green papaya salad, tangy and crunchy
ingredients:
- 400g green papaya, peeled and julienned (or shredded)
- 3 tbsp small dried shrimp (tôm khô)
- Large handful Vietnamese basil and/or mint
- 1 long red chili, sliced
- For dressing: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 2 tsp sugar, 1 clove garlic minced
- Crushed roasted peanuts
- Fried shallots
instructions:
1. Soak dried shrimp in warm water 10 minutes, drain.
2. Make dressing: mix lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and garlic until sugar dissolves.
3. Toss green papaya in a bowl with dressing. Massage gently.
4. Add dried shrimp, herbs, and chili. Toss well.
5. Taste — adjust fish sauce for salt, lime for sour, sugar for balance.
6. Plate and top with peanuts and fried shallots.
tips: Use a mandoline or box grater for uniform thin shreds. The papaya needs to be very green and unripe — you want crunchy, not soft.

name: Bánh Flan
description: A silky, dense caramel custard — slightly richer than French crème caramel and distinctly Vietnamese in its creaminess from condensed milk. Steamed or baked in individual cups. The dark caramel pools at the bottom and cascades over when unmolded.
tagline: Trembling custard with caramel — French left behind
ingredients:
- For caramel: 80g sugar + 2 tbsp water
- 3 eggs + 2 egg yolks
- 1 can (395g) sweetened condensed milk
- 1 cup whole milk
- 1 tsp vanilla extract
instructions:
1. Make caramel: cook sugar and water in a pan until deep amber. Immediately pour into the bottom of 6 ramekins. Swirl to coat. Let set.
2. Whisk eggs and yolks gently — don't create bubbles.
3. Warm condensed milk and whole milk together until just hot. Slowly pour into egg mixture, whisking gently.
4. Add vanilla. Strain through a fine mesh to remove any foam or threads.
5. Pour over set caramel in ramekins.
6. To steam: place in a steamer, cover lid with a towel to prevent condensation dripping. Steam on low for 25–30 minutes until just set.
7. Cool completely, then refrigerate at least 2 hours.
8. To unmold: run a knife around the edge, invert onto a plate.
tips: The texture should wobble like Jell-O when you tap the ramekin but not be liquid. Overbaking creates holes and a rubbery texture.

name: Chè Đậu Đỏ
description: Whole red azuki beans simmered low with palm sugar and a pandan leaf until soft and gently sweet. Served warm or spooned over shaved ice with coconut cream drizzled on top. Simple, comforting, and fragrant — one of the most beloved Vietnamese desserts.
tagline: Red bean dessert soup, sweetened with rock sugar
ingredients:
- 250g dried red azuki beans, soaked overnight
- 80g palm sugar (or brown sugar), or more to taste
- 2 pandan leaves, tied in a knot
- 1L water
- Pinch of salt
- For coconut cream topping: 100ml coconut cream + pinch of salt + 1 tsp tapioca starch mixed in — heat gently until slightly thickened
instructions:
1. Drain soaked beans. Add to pot with 1 liter fresh water and pandan leaves.
2. Bring to a boil, then simmer on medium-low for 45–60 minutes until beans are fully soft but still holding their shape.
3. Add palm sugar and salt. Simmer 10 more minutes. Taste — adjust sweetness.
4. Meanwhile, gently heat coconut cream with salt and tapioca starch until just thickened.
5. Serve beans warm in bowls, drizzled with a spoonful of salted coconut cream.
tips: The pandan leaf is not optional — it's what gives this dessert its distinct grassy fragrance. If you can't find fresh pandan, use a drop of extract.

name: Chè Ba Màu
description: The iconic Vietnamese layered dessert — a glass of yellow mung bean paste, green pandan jelly, and red azuki beans, topped with shaved ice and coconut cream. Each layer has its own texture: starchy, slippery, chewy. Best eaten quickly before the ice melts.
tagline: Three-layer sweet soup, a rainbow in a glass
ingredients:
- For mung bean layer (yellow): 150g split mung beans, soaked 2 hours, steamed until soft, mashed with 2 tbsp sugar and a knob of coconut oil
- For pandan jelly (green): 2g agar-agar powder + 250ml water + 1 tbsp pandan juice (blend 5 pandan leaves with water, strain) + 2 tbsp sugar
- For red bean layer: 150g cooked sweetened azuki beans (from chè đậu đỏ recipe)
- For coconut cream topping: 150ml coconut cream + pinch of salt, warmed
- Shaved ice or crushed ice
instructions:
1. Make pandan jelly: dissolve agar in water, add pandan juice and sugar, bring to boil, pour into tray. Cool and set, then cut into small cubes or strips.
2. Have mung bean paste, red beans, and coconut cream all prepared and at hand.
3. Assemble glasses: start with a layer of red beans, then mung bean paste, then pandan jelly pieces.
4. Pack shaved ice over the top.
5. Drizzle generously with salted coconut cream.
6. Serve immediately with a long spoon.
tips: Make each component separately — this is a dish of parts assembled at the last minute. The beauty is in the layers staying distinct until the spoon mixes them.

name: Bánh Chuối Nướng
description: Sliced bananas baked into a dense, sticky coconut milk custard until the top caramelizes and the inside becomes fragrant with pandan. More pudding than cake. Eaten warm, eaten cold, eaten for every occasion.
tagline: Banana cake, baked in banana leaf
ingredients:
- 4 ripe bananas, sliced into coins
- 200ml coconut milk
- 100ml coconut cream
- 80g sugar
- 100g glutinous rice flour
- 1 tbsp tapioca starch
- 2 pandan leaves, tied in a knot
- Pinch of salt
- Sesame seeds (optional, for top)
instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 180°C. Grease a 20cm baking dish.
2. Heat coconut milk with pandan leaves and sugar until sugar dissolves. Remove pandan.
3. Whisk in glutinous rice flour, tapioca starch, and salt until smooth.
4. Fold banana slices into the batter gently.
5. Pour into baking dish. Scatter sesame seeds on top if using.
6. Bake 45–50 minutes until the top is deep golden and the center is set but jiggly.
7. Let cool at least 20 minutes before slicing — it firms as it cools.
tips: Use very ripe bananas — spotted and sweet. Under-ripe bananas won't caramelize or add enough flavor.

name: Cà Phê Sữa Đá
description: Strong Robusta coffee brewed through a small metal drip filter (phin) directly over sweetened condensed milk and a glass of ice. Slow, intentional, and absolutely worth the 10 minutes it takes to drip. The condensed milk swirls in dark and slow. The contrast of bitter and sweet over ice is the point.
tagline: Dark drip, sweet condensed milk, poured over ice
ingredients:
- 2 tbsp Vietnamese Robusta ground coffee (coarsely ground)
- 2–3 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- Hot water (not boiling — around 90°C)
- Glass of ice
instructions:
1. Add condensed milk to the bottom of a glass or small cup.
2. Set the phin filter on top. Add coffee grounds, press the filter plate down gently.
3. Pour a small amount of hot water to bloom the coffee. Wait 30 seconds.
4. Fill the filter with hot water. Place the lid on and wait — the coffee should drip slowly, 6–8 minutes total.
5. Once dripped through, stir the coffee and condensed milk together in the small cup.
6. Pour over the glass of ice. Stir and drink.
tips: Vietnamese coffee brands like Trung Nguyên or Café Du Monde (with chicory) will give the right bitter, earthy profile. Don't rush the drip.

name: Cà Phê Trứng
description: A Hà Nội invention from the 1940s — strong brewed coffee topped with a thick, frothy cream made from egg yolks whipped with condensed milk and a little vanilla. The result sits somewhere between a drink and a dessert. Rich enough to eat with a spoon, but you drink it anyway.
tagline: Whipped egg yolk on hot coffee — Hà Nội's invention
ingredients:
- 2 egg yolks (room temperature)
- 3 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- ½ tsp vanilla extract
- 1 cup strong brewed Vietnamese coffee (or espresso)
- Optional: pinch of salt, pinch of cinnamon
instructions:
1. Brew coffee and keep warm.
2. Using a hand mixer or whisk, beat egg yolks, condensed milk, vanilla, and a pinch of salt for 3–5 minutes until thick, pale, and ribbon-like. The mixture should hold its shape when you lift the beater.
3. Pour warm (not boiling) coffee into a small cup or glass.
4. Spoon the egg cream generously over the top — it should sit as a thick, billowing layer.
5. Dust with cinnamon if using. Drink by sipping through the cream.
tips: The egg cream must be whipped to a proper thick foam or it will dissolve into the coffee. Don't rush the beating step.

name: Sinh Tố Bơ
description: Ripe avocado blended with condensed milk, whole milk, and ice until thick and pale green. Vietnamese street drink that exists in the dessert zone. Creamy, sweet, and richly satisfying in a single tall glass.
tagline: Thick avocado blended with condensed milk
ingredients:
- 1 very ripe avocado, pitted and scooped
- 3 tbsp sweetened condensed milk (more to taste)
- 100ml whole milk
- 8–10 ice cubes
- Optional: 1 scoop vanilla ice cream for extra richness
instructions:
1. Add avocado, condensed milk, milk, and ice to a blender.
2. Blend on high until completely smooth and thick.
3. Taste — add more condensed milk if you want it sweeter.
4. Pour into a tall glass. Serve immediately with a straw.
tips: The avocado must be fully ripe — slightly soft, dark-skinned. An underripe avocado will be bitter and refuse to blend smooth.

name: Nước Chanh Muối
description: A tall glass of fresh lime juice, salt, sugar, and cold water. The salt is the move — it rounds out the sour and makes the drink more refreshing than plain limeade. Best drunk at a plastic table with a bowl of phở, or outside in the heat.
tagline: Salted lemonade — sour, salty, surprisingly refreshing
ingredients:
- Juice of 2–3 limes (about 60ml)
- ½ tsp fine salt
- 1 tbsp sugar, or to taste
- 200ml cold still or sparkling water
- Ice
- A few mint leaves (optional)
instructions:
1. Add lime juice, salt, and sugar to a glass.
2. Stir until salt and sugar dissolve.
3. Add ice.
4. Pour cold water over. Stir gently.
5. Taste — adjust salt, lime, or sugar. It should be refreshingly sour with just a hint of sweetness and a salinity that makes you want another sip.
6. Add mint if using.
tips: The ratio to remember is sour-forward with salt support. Start conservative with salt and add more — it's easy to over-salt and hard to correct.

name: Bánh Canh Gà
description: Thick, chewy tapioca noodles in a rich chicken broth — bánh canh is the comforting, starchy cousin of phở. The noodles are slippery and substantial, and the broth is fortified with chicken bones and a little fish sauce until deeply savory.
tagline: Thick noodles in a rich, slow-simmered broth
ingredients:
- 500g tapioca noodles (bánh canh) or thick udon as substitute
- 1 whole chicken (or 4 thighs)
- 1.5L water
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar
- 3 shallots, charred
- 2cm ginger, bruised
- White pepper
- To serve: sliced scallions, cilantro, fried shallots, chili, lime
instructions:
1. Simmer chicken in water with charred shallots, ginger, fish sauce, salt, and sugar for 40–50 minutes until cooked through.
2. Remove chicken. Shred meat by hand. Discard skin and bones.
3. Strain broth. Return to pot and bring to boil.
4. Add tapioca noodles directly to broth, simmer 5–8 minutes until just cooked and translucent.
5. Ladle into bowls with shredded chicken. Top with scallions, cilantro, fried shallots, white pepper.
6. Serve with lime and chili on the side.
tips: Bánh canh noodles are fragile — don't overcook. They should be chewy with a slight resistance, not mushy.

name: Miến Gà
description: Silky glass noodles in a clear, golden chicken broth — light, clean, and restorative. The cellophane noodles absorb the broth and become almost transparent. A Hà Nội breakfast staple and the go-to meal when someone is under the weather.
tagline: Glass noodle soup, clear and deeply comforting
ingredients:
- 200g glass noodles (miến), soaked in cold water 20 minutes
- 1 whole chicken or 4 bone-in thighs
- 1.5L water
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar
- 3cm ginger, sliced
- 2 shallots
- Wood ear mushrooms, soaked and sliced (optional)
- Fried shallots, sliced scallions, white pepper, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Simmer chicken with ginger and shallots in water for 35–40 minutes. Remove chicken, shred the meat.
2. Strain broth. Season with fish sauce, salt, sugar. Bring back to boil.
3. Add wood ear mushrooms if using, simmer 5 minutes.
4. Drain soaked glass noodles. Add to boiling broth for 2–3 minutes until just cooked.
5. Serve in bowls with shredded chicken, fried shallots, scallions, and white pepper.
tips: Don't soak the glass noodles too long or they'll fall apart in the broth. Cold water, 20 minutes — no more.

name: Xôi Xéo
description: Sticky rice topped with a crumble of steamed and dry-fried mung bean, fried shallots, and a drizzle of scallion oil. A beloved Hà Nội street breakfast — sold from trays balanced on vendors' heads in the early morning hours.
tagline: Golden sticky rice, mung bean paste, eaten at dawn
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 150g split mung beans, soaked 2 hours
- 1 tsp turmeric powder
- 1 tsp salt
- For scallion oil: 3 scallion stalks, sliced + 3 tbsp neutral oil, heated until smoking
- Fried shallots
instructions:
1. Drain soaked glutinous rice. Toss with turmeric and salt.
2. Steam rice for 35–40 minutes until cooked and sticky.
3. Meanwhile, steam mung beans until completely soft, 25–30 minutes. Mash lightly and season with a pinch of salt.
4. In a dry pan over medium heat, stir the mashed mung bean until it dries out slightly and becomes crumbly. Remove from heat.
5. Make scallion oil: heat oil until smoking, pour over sliced scallions.
6. Serve rice in a mound. Top generously with mung bean crumble, fried shallots, and scallion oil.
tips: The mung bean should be dry and crumbly, not wet — the stir-drying step is crucial. Too much moisture and it clumps.

name: Hủ Tiếu Nam Vang
description: A Sài Gòn noodle soup with Cambodian roots — rice noodles in a light pork and dried shrimp broth, topped with sliced pork, shrimp, quail eggs, and crispy shallots. Clean, sweet, and deeply savory. Can be served in broth (nước) or dry (khô) with broth on the side.
tagline: Saigon's street-corner noodle, eaten standing up
ingredients:
- 500g flat rice noodles or hủ tiếu noodles
- 500g pork neck or pork ribs
- 1.5L water
- 30g dried shrimp
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar, 1 tsp salt
- 200g medium shrimp, peeled
- 100g pork liver, sliced thin (optional)
- 6–8 quail eggs, boiled and peeled
- Fried shallots, sliced scallions, chili, lime, bean sprouts, garlic chives
instructions:
1. Simmer pork bones and dried shrimp in water for 45 minutes. Skim foam. Season with fish sauce, salt, sugar.
2. Remove bones and pull any attached pork. Slice pork thinly.
3. Poach shrimp and pork liver briefly in the hot broth. Remove.
4. Cook noodles according to package, rinse with cold water.
5. Assemble bowls: noodles, pork slices, shrimp, quail eggs, scallions, fried shallots.
6. Ladle hot broth over. Serve with bean sprouts, chili, lime on the side.
tips: For the dry version, toss noodles with a little oyster sauce and dark soy before serving. Broth is served on the side for dipping and sipping.

name: Bún Mắm
description: The funkiest noodle soup in Vietnam — a Mekong Delta specialty built on a base of fermented fish paste (mắm cá) that gives the broth an intense, complex depth unlike anything else. Rich with pork belly, shrimp, and eggplant, softened by coconut milk, and served with an enormous platter of fresh herbs.
tagline: The boldest broth in the south
ingredients:
- 150g mắm cá (fermented fish paste), or mắm prawn
- 1L pork stock
- 200g pork belly, boiled and sliced
- 200g large shrimp, peeled
- 1 small eggplant, cut into wedges
- 100ml coconut milk
- 2 stalks lemongrass, bruised
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar
- 500g thick rice vermicelli (bún)
- To serve: water spinach, bean sprouts, banana blossom, fresh herbs, lime, chili
instructions:
1. Dilute mắm in 200ml warm water, strain to remove solids. Reserve liquid.
2. Bring pork stock to boil. Add strained mắm liquid and lemongrass. Simmer 20 minutes.
3. Add eggplant, cook 10 minutes.
4. Add coconut milk, pork belly. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
5. Add shrimp in the last 3–4 minutes.
6. Cook bún noodles, divide into bowls. Ladle broth and toppings over.
7. Serve with a large platter of fresh herbs and vegetables.
tips: The mắm is very strong — start with less and add to taste. The coconut milk balances the funk. Don't skip the fresh herbs; they're essential, not garnish.

name: Mì Quảng
description: Quảng Nam's signature dish — thick turmeric-yellow noodles in just a small amount of rich, concentrated broth (not a soup, more of a dressed noodle), topped with pork, shrimp, quail eggs, peanuts, sesame rice crackers, and a mountain of fresh greens. Every bowl is different, every family has their version.
tagline: Central Vietnam's most iconic bowl
ingredients:
- 400g fresh mì Quảng noodles (or wide rice noodles tossed with ½ tsp turmeric)
- 300g pork shoulder, thinly sliced
- 200g shrimp, peeled
- 3 shallots, sliced
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp annatto oil, 1 tsp sugar
- 400ml pork or shrimp stock
- 4 quail eggs, boiled
- Roasted peanuts, crushed
- Sesame rice crackers (bánh tráng)
- Fresh herbs: banana blossom, mint, perilla, bean sprouts, scallions
instructions:
1. Stir-fry shallots and garlic in annatto oil. Add pork and shrimp, cook 2–3 minutes.
2. Add fish sauce, sugar, and stock. Simmer 10 minutes — the broth should be concentrated and flavorful, not watery.
3. Cook noodles per package. Divide into bowls.
4. Ladle a small amount of broth and toppings over noodles — just enough to dress, not submerge.
5. Top with quail eggs, peanuts, and fried shallots.
6. Serve with fresh herbs, sesame crackers on the side.
tips: Mì Quảng uses far less broth than typical Vietnamese noodle soups — the dish should be almost dry, with broth acting as a dressing. More is not more here.

name: Cơm Chiên Dương Châu
description: Vietnamese-Chinese fried rice with eggs, char siu pork, shrimp, green peas, and scallions — a staple of Sài Gòn Chinese-Vietnamese restaurants. The key is day-old rice and a very hot wok.
tagline: Fried rice the whole neighborhood can smell
ingredients:
- 2 cups cooked jasmine rice, day-old and cold
- 2 eggs, beaten
- 100g char siu pork (or Chinese BBQ pork), diced
- 100g shrimp, peeled and roughly chopped
- 60g frozen peas
- 3 scallions, sliced
- 2 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp oyster sauce
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- 2 tbsp neutral oil with high smoke point
- Salt and white pepper
instructions:
1. Break up cold rice with wet hands to separate any clumps.
2. Heat wok over highest heat until smoking. Add oil.
3. Add shrimp and pork, stir-fry 1 minute. Push to the side.
4. Pour in beaten eggs, scramble briefly until just set.
5. Add rice. Toss everything together, pressing against the wok to get heat on the grains.
6. Add peas, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Toss vigorously for 2 minutes.
7. Finish with sesame oil, scallions, and white pepper.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — fresh rice steams and clumps. The wok must be screaming hot for wok hei (the smoky, charred quality you want).

name: Rau Muống Xào Tỏi
description: Water spinach stir-fried over high heat with garlic until wilted and fragrant — one of the simplest, most essential Vietnamese side dishes that appears on every home table. The stems stay slightly crunchy; the leaves wilt and melt. Done in under 5 minutes.
tagline: Morning glory, flash-fried with garlic — simple perfection
ingredients:
- 400g water spinach (rau muống), trimmed and cut into 5cm lengths
- 5 cloves garlic, thinly sliced
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- 1 tbsp fish sauce (or soy sauce for vegetarian)
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Wash water spinach thoroughly. Drain but don't dry completely — the water helps it steam.
2. Heat wok over very high heat until smoking. Add oil.
3. Add garlic, stir-fry 20 seconds until fragrant and just beginning to color.
4. Add water spinach all at once. Toss quickly and constantly.
5. Add fish sauce. Continue tossing until stems are tender-crisp and leaves are wilted, about 3 minutes total.
6. Taste, adjust salt. Serve immediately.
tips: High heat and speed are everything. Water spinach releases a lot of liquid — you want it to evaporate quickly, not pool at the bottom of the pan.

name: Đậu Hũ Sốt Cà Chua
description: Silken tofu pan-fried until golden, then simmered in a simple tomato sauce with scallions and a touch of fish sauce. A beloved home dish that's fast, cheap, and deeply satisfying — Vietnamese comfort food that happens to be vegetarian.
tagline: Tofu in tomato sauce, a weekday staple
ingredients:
- 400g firm tofu, drained, patted dry, cut into rectangles
- 3 ripe tomatoes, diced
- 4 scallions, cut into 3cm lengths
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce (or soy sauce)
- 1 tsp sugar
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Salt and pepper
instructions:
1. Fry tofu in oil over medium-high heat until golden on all sides, 5–6 minutes total. Remove and set aside.
2. In the same pan, fry garlic until fragrant, 30 seconds.
3. Add tomatoes. Cook over medium-high heat, stirring occasionally, until broken down and saucy, 5–6 minutes.
4. Season with fish sauce, sugar, salt, and pepper.
5. Return tofu to the pan. Toss gently in the sauce.
6. Add scallions, stir once, and serve over rice.
tips: Pat the tofu very dry before frying — moisture is the enemy of a good golden crust. Use a non-stick or well-seasoned pan.

name: Cá Hấp Gừng Hành
description: Whole fish steamed over a plate of ginger and scallion, then finished with sizzling hot oil poured over the top — the same technique used across Vietnamese and Chinese cooking to perfume the fish at the very last second. Clean, simple, and exactly as good as the fish you start with.
tagline: Steamed fish with ginger and spring onion
ingredients:
- 1 whole sea bass, snapper, or tilapia (500–700g), cleaned and scored
- 5cm ginger, half sliced into coins, half julienned
- 4 scallion stalks, half cut into 4cm lengths, half julienned
- 2 tbsp light soy sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 3 tbsp neutral oil
- Fresh cilantro to serve
instructions:
1. Score fish on both sides 3–4 times through to the bone.
2. Place sliced ginger and scallion lengths on a plate. Lay fish on top.
3. Steam over boiling water for 10–14 minutes depending on thickness. Fish should flake easily at the thickest point.
4. Mix soy sauce and sugar. Pour over the fish.
5. Pile julienned ginger and scallion on top of the fish.
6. Heat oil in a small pan until smoking. Immediately pour over the ginger and scallion — it will sizzle and spit.
7. Garnish with cilantro. Serve at once.
tips: The flash of smoking oil at the end is what finishes the dish — it cooks the raw garnish and perfumes the fish. Don't skip it or use lukewarm oil.

name: Tôm Hấp Sả
description: Live or very fresh shrimp steamed over lemongrass, beer, and aromatics until just cooked — sweet, firm, and fragrant. Eaten shell-on with salt, pepper, and lime dipping sauce. A dish where technique is simply: don't overcook.
tagline: Steamed shrimp with lemongrass — pure and clean
ingredients:
- 500g large shrimp, shell-on
- 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised and cut in half
- 1 can (330ml) beer or 330ml water
- 3cm ginger, sliced
- For dipping sauce: 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp black pepper, juice of 1 lime — mix together
- Fresh chili (optional)
instructions:
1. Pour beer into a pot. Add lemongrass and ginger. Bring to a boil.
2. Place shrimp in a steamer basket above the liquid (not submerged).
3. Cover and steam 4–6 minutes until shrimp are pink, opaque, and just curled. Don't let them curl into tight circles — that means overcooked.
4. Remove immediately. Arrange on a plate.
5. Mix salt, pepper, and lime juice in small ramekins.
6. Eat shrimp shell-on, dipping in the salt-pepper-lime sauce.
tips: The moment the shrimp turns fully pink and the flesh becomes opaque is exactly when to stop. Remove from heat 30 seconds before you think they're done.

name: Sườn Nướng
description: Pork spare ribs marinated in lemongrass, fish sauce, and honey, then grilled over charcoal until lacquered and charred at the bone ends. A staple of Vietnamese BBQ spreads and street food stalls — best eaten with cold beer and good company.
tagline: Grilled ribs that belong at every family meal
ingredients:
- 1kg pork spare ribs, cut into individual ribs
- 3 stalks lemongrass, white parts minced
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 3 shallots, minced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tbsp honey or sugar
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp five spice powder
- 1 tsp turmeric
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
instructions:
1. Combine lemongrass, garlic, shallots, fish sauce, honey, soy, five spice, turmeric, and oil. Mix well.
2. Score the ribs and marinate in the mixture for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
3. Grill over medium-high heat, turning every 5 minutes, for 25–35 minutes total.
4. Brush with extra honey in the last 5 minutes for a lacquered finish.
5. Rest 5 minutes before serving.
tips: Low and slow on the grill prevents burning. The sugar in the marinade will char if the heat is too high. Use indirect heat for the first 20 minutes, then direct for the last 10.

name: Vịt Nấu Chao
description: Duck braised in fermented tofu (chao) — one of Southern Vietnam's most unusual and beloved dishes. The fermented tofu melts into the sauce, creating a funky, creamy, savory coating for the duck that is somehow entirely its own thing. Served with crusty bread.
tagline: Duck braised in fermented tofu, rich and complex
ingredients:
- 1 duck, cut into 8 pieces
- 3–4 cubes red fermented tofu (chao đỏ) + 2 tbsp of the brine
- 3 stalks lemongrass, bruised
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 3 shallots, sliced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- 200ml water or coconut water
- Crusty baguette and fresh herbs to serve
instructions:
1. Mash fermented tofu with its brine into a paste.
2. Brown duck pieces in batches in hot oil until golden. Remove.
3. In same pot, fry garlic and shallots until soft. Add lemongrass.
4. Add duck back in. Add fermented tofu paste, fish sauce, sugar, and water.
5. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a very low simmer. Cover and braise 60–75 minutes until duck is very tender.
6. Uncover, increase heat to reduce sauce slightly.
7. Serve with torn baguette for dipping.
tips: The fermented tofu will smell intense — trust the process. It mellows significantly during braising and transforms into a deeply savory sauce.

name: Cơm Hến
description: Huế's famous clam rice — tiny freshwater clams stir-fried with lemongrass, sesame peanuts, and herbs, served cold over cold cooked rice, with the hot clam broth poured on the side or over the top. A textural contradiction that somehow works perfectly.
tagline: Tiny clams over rice, a Huế specialty
ingredients:
- 400g fresh clams (or small mussels), scrubbed
- 2 cups cold cooked rice
- 1 stalk lemongrass, minced
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp shrimp paste (mắm ruốc)
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp chili flakes
- 1 tsp sugar
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Fresh herbs: perilla, mint, banana blossom, bean sprouts
- Roasted peanuts, sesame seeds, fried shallots
instructions:
1. Steam clams until just opened, 3–4 minutes. Remove meat. Reserve broth.
2. In a wok over high heat, stir-fry lemongrass and garlic in oil. Add clam meat.
3. Add shrimp paste, fish sauce, sugar, and chili flakes. Toss 2–3 minutes until fragrant and slightly dry.
4. Strain clam broth, bring to boil, season lightly.
5. In each bowl: cold rice first. Pile clam stir-fry on top. Add herbs, peanuts, sesame, fried shallots.
6. Pour hot broth on top (or on the side) at the table.
tips: The contrast of cold rice and hot broth is intentional — it is the dish. Don't serve this warm or you lose what makes it special.

name: Bún Mọc
description: A clear, light pork bone broth with bouncy handmade pork balls (mọc), sliced pork, and ear mushrooms, served over rice vermicelli. Mọc are the Vietnamese meatball — seasoned pork blended with wood ear mushroom and vermicelli, then formed and simmered directly in the broth.
tagline: Pork meatball soup, quiet and nourishing
ingredients:
- For broth: 600g pork neck bones, 1.5L water, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp sugar, shallots, ginger
- For mọc: 300g ground pork, 20g glass noodles (soaked, chopped), 15g dried wood ear mushroom (soaked, minced), 1 egg, 1 tbsp fish sauce, white pepper
- 400g rice vermicelli (bún), cooked
- To serve: scallions, fried shallots, cilantro, lime, chili
instructions:
1. Simmer pork bones with ginger and shallots in water for 45 minutes. Strain and season broth.
2. Mix ground pork with glass noodles, wood ear mushroom, egg, fish sauce, and pepper until sticky.
3. Wet hands, roll into balls the size of a large grape. Set aside.
4. Bring broth to a gentle simmer. Drop mọc in and poach for 8–10 minutes until cooked through and floating.
5. Divide cooked bún into bowls. Ladle broth and mọc over.
6. Top with scallions, fried shallots, and cilantro.
tips: The mọc should be dense and bouncy, not soft and mushy. Mixing the meat vigorously until it becomes sticky is what gives them their characteristic texture.

name: Bò Nướng Lá Lốt
description: Ground beef seasoned with lemongrass and shrimp paste, rolled in wild betel leaves and grilled until the leaves char and crinkle at the edges. The leaf adds a distinctly peppery, slightly medicinal fragrance that is completely addictive. Eaten with rice paper, fresh herbs, and nước chấm.
tagline: Grilled beef in betel leaves — smoky and aromatic
ingredients:
- 400g ground beef
- 20–25 fresh lá lốt (wild betel leaves)
- 2 stalks lemongrass, minced
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 shallots, minced
- 1 tbsp mắm ruốc (shrimp paste)
- 1 tsp sugar, salt and pepper
- To serve: rice paper, lettuce, cucumber, mint, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Mix beef with lemongrass, garlic, shallots, shrimp paste, sugar, salt, and pepper until well combined.
2. Place a lá lốt leaf shiny side down. Spoon a heaping tablespoon of filling near the stem end. Roll tightly, secure with a toothpick.
3. Grill over high heat, turning once, 3–4 minutes per side until the leaf is charred and the beef is cooked through.
4. Serve with rice paper, fresh herbs, and nước chấm for wrapping and dipping.
tips: The leaf must char slightly — the bitterness that develops from charring is part of the flavor. Don't grill on too low heat or the leaf steams instead of chars.

name: Cá Chiên Sốt Cà
description: Whole fish or fish fillets fried until crispy, then served with a sweet, sharp tomato and scallion sauce. A quick weeknight dinner that looks more impressive than it is — the crispy fish and bright sauce are a classic pairing.
tagline: Fried fish in tomato sauce, tangy and vibrant
ingredients:
- 2 fish fillets (tilapia, catfish, or sea bass), patted dry
- 3 ripe tomatoes, diced
- 3 scallions, cut into 3cm pieces
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- Neutral oil for frying
- Salt and pepper, cornstarch for dusting
instructions:
1. Season fish with salt and pepper. Dust lightly with cornstarch.
2. Fry in 1cm of hot oil over medium-high heat until golden and crispy, 4–5 minutes per side. Drain on paper towels.
3. Pour off most oil, leaving 1 tbsp. Fry garlic until fragrant.
4. Add tomatoes and cook until soft and jammy, 5–6 minutes.
5. Add fish sauce, sugar, and scallions. Stir briefly.
6. Pour sauce over crispy fish. Serve immediately with rice.
tips: Serve the sauce over the fish right before eating — the crust loses its crispiness quickly once sauced.

name: Gà Rôti
description: Vietnamese-French rotisserie chicken — marinated in soy, honey, and five spice, then roasted until the skin turns deep mahogany and lacquered. A Chinese-French-Vietnamese hybrid that has become entirely its own thing in Sài Gòn, sold from rotisserie shops with rice and pickled vegetables.
tagline: Vietnamese roast chicken, golden and fragrant
ingredients:
- 1 whole chicken (1.4–1.6kg)
- 3 tbsp soy sauce
- 2 tbsp honey
- 1 tbsp oyster sauce
- 1 tsp five spice powder
- 1 tsp garlic powder
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
instructions:
1. Mix all marinade ingredients. Rub generously all over the chicken, including under the skin.
2. Marinate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight in the fridge, uncovered (drying the skin).
3. Preheat oven to 200°C. Roast chicken breast-side up for 50–60 minutes until skin is deep mahogany and juices run clear.
4. Brush with extra honey in the last 10 minutes.
5. Rest 10 minutes before carving.
6. Serve with steamed rice, đồ chua, and maggi sauce.
tips: Leaving the chicken uncovered overnight in the fridge dries the skin and leads to better lacquering. The honey in the last 10 minutes gives that deep, sticky glaze.

name: Canh Bí Đỏ Nấu Tôm
description: Pumpkin and shrimp soup — one of the most common everyday canh dishes in Southern Vietnamese households. Sweet from the pumpkin, savory from the shrimp, and seasoned with just fish sauce and pepper. On the table in 20 minutes.
tagline: Sweet pumpkin, sweet shrimp, 20 minutes
ingredients:
- 400g kabocha or butternut pumpkin, peeled and cut into 3cm chunks
- 200g shrimp, peeled (shells reserved)
- 800ml water
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- Salt and white pepper
- 2 scallions, sliced
instructions:
1. Simmer shrimp shells in water for 10 minutes. Strain to make quick shrimp stock.
2. Add pumpkin to stock. Simmer 10–12 minutes until just tender.
3. Add shrimp. Cook 2–3 minutes until pink and just cooked.
4. Season with fish sauce, salt, and white pepper.
5. Scatter scallions on top. Serve alongside rice.
tips: Don't overcook the shrimp — add them in the last 2–3 minutes and pull off the heat promptly. The pumpkin should be tender but not falling apart.

name: Súp Măng Cua
description: Asparagus and crab soup — a Vietnamese-French fusion dish from the colonial era that has become thoroughly Vietnamese. Silky from cornstarch thickening, rich from crab, and fragrant with egg white ribbons. A staple at Vietnamese wedding banquets.
tagline: A colonial-era soup that became forever Vietnamese
ingredients:
- 200g crab meat (fresh, canned, or imitation)
- 1 can white asparagus, drained and cut into 2cm pieces (or fresh)
- 1L chicken stock
- 2 egg whites, lightly beaten
- 3 tbsp cornstarch dissolved in 4 tbsp cold water
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar, white pepper
- 2 scallions, thinly sliced
instructions:
1. Bring stock to a boil. Add fish sauce, sugar, and asparagus. Simmer 3 minutes.
2. Add crab meat. Stir to break apart slightly.
3. Give cornstarch mixture a stir, then slowly pour into the soup while stirring. The soup will thicken immediately.
4. Reduce heat. Slowly pour beaten egg whites in a thin stream while stirring gently — this creates egg ribbons.
5. Season with white pepper. Serve topped with scallions.
tips: Add the egg white slowly and stir in one direction only to get clean ribbons. Fast or circular stirring breaks the ribbons into cloudy bits.

name: Dưa Cải
description: Fermented mustard greens — tangy, slightly sour, and crunchy — served alongside thịt kho tàu and fatty braised dishes to cut through richness. A week-long fermentation project that rewards patience. Essential on the Tết table.
tagline: Fermented mustard greens — sour, pungent, perfect with pork
ingredients:
- 600g mustard greens (cải bẹ xanh), cut into 5cm pieces
- 1 tbsp salt
- 1 tsp sugar
- For brine: 500ml water, 1.5 tbsp salt, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 tbsp rice vinegar
instructions:
1. Toss mustard greens with salt and sugar. Let sit 30 minutes until wilted.
2. Squeeze firmly and discard excess liquid. Rinse under cold water.
3. Make brine: combine water, salt, sugar, and vinegar. Stir to dissolve.
4. Pack greens tightly into a clean glass jar. Pour brine over to submerge.
5. Weigh down with a small plate or bag of water. Leave at room temperature 2–4 days until pleasantly sour.
6. Refrigerate once fermented. Keeps for 1–2 weeks.
tips: Taste from day 2. The fermentation time depends on temperature — warmer rooms ferment faster. You want sour but not mushy.

name: Canh Rau Ngót
description: A simple, clean soup made from rau ngót leaves — a slightly bitter, iron-rich leafy green — simmered in a light pork broth with minced pork or shrimp. An everyday household soup, unremarkable in description but deeply comforting in reality.
tagline: A green leaf soup that tastes like home
ingredients:
- 200g rau ngót leaves (or substitute spinach + a little fresh sorrel)
- 200g ground pork or peeled shrimp
- 800ml pork stock or water
- 1 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 shallot, minced
- Salt and white pepper
instructions:
1. Season ground pork with a little fish sauce and pepper.
2. Bring stock to a boil. Add shallot and ground pork, stirring to break into small pieces. Cook 5 minutes.
3. Add rau ngót leaves.
4. Season with fish sauce, salt, and white pepper.
5. Simmer 2–3 minutes until leaves are just wilted and bright green.
6. Serve alongside rice and a main dish.
tips: Don't overcook rau ngót — it should be vibrant green and slightly tender, not grey and mushy. Add it at the very end.

name: Bánh Tráng Nướng
description: A rice paper sheet toasted over charcoal or an open flame until crispy, then loaded with egg, scallion oil, dried shrimp, and chili sauce — Đà Lạt's famous street food, sometimes called 'Vietnamese pizza.' Thin, crackly, and eaten in two minutes standing up.
tagline: Grilled rice paper with egg and toppings — street food gold
ingredients:
- 1 large rice paper sheet (bánh tráng)
- 1 egg
- 1 tbsp scallion oil (mỡ hành)
- 1 tbsp dried shrimp, soaked briefly and drained
- Sriracha or chili sauce
- Maggi seasoning sauce
- Fried shallots (optional)
instructions:
1. Place rice paper sheet directly over low gas flame or on a wire rack over coals.
2. Heat until it starts to blister and become slightly crispy, turning with tongs.
3. Crack egg onto the center of the sheet. Spread with the back of a spoon.
4. Continue toasting over low heat until egg is just set, 2–3 minutes.
5. Drizzle with scallion oil, scatter dried shrimp and fried shallots.
6. Add sriracha and Maggi to taste. Fold or eat flat immediately.
tips: Work quickly — the rice paper crisps fast over direct flame. Too high heat and it burns before the egg sets. Low and patient.

name: Bánh Tráng Trộn
description: Shredded rice paper tossed with green mango, dried beef, quail eggs, and a tart tamarind dressing. A Sài Gòn street snack that teenagers eat in plastic bags after school — crunchy, sour, sweet, and spicy all at once.
tagline: Rice paper tossed with everything good
ingredients:
- 150g dried rice paper (bánh tráng), cut into strips
- 1 green mango, julienned (or green papaya)
- 50g dried beef (bò khô), shredded
- 4 quail eggs, boiled and halved
- 2 tbsp tamarind paste dissolved in 2 tbsp warm water
- 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, chili sauce
- Roasted peanuts, fried shallots, Vietnamese coriander (rau răm)
instructions:
1. Make dressing: mix tamarind water, fish sauce, sugar, and chili until sugar dissolves.
2. In a large bowl, toss rice paper strips with green mango and dried beef.
3. Pour dressing over and toss vigorously until everything is coated and the rice paper softens slightly but retains some chew.
4. Add quail eggs, rau răm, and peanuts. Toss once more.
5. Pile into bowls or eat from the bag immediately.
tips: Eat this fast — the rice paper goes from pleasantly chewy to soggy within 10 minutes. Mix and eat, don't let it sit.

name: Bánh Bèo
description: Bite-sized steamed rice cakes in shallow saucers, topped with dried shrimp floss, crispy pork skin, and scallion oil — a Huế specialty eaten as a snack or light meal. Each tiny cake is a single mouthful: the soft, yielding rice against the crunch of pork skin.
tagline: Steamed rice cakes, floating in fish sauce
ingredients:
- For batter: 200g rice flour, 2 tbsp tapioca starch, ½ tsp salt, 500ml water — mix smooth
- 100g small dried shrimp, soaked, drained and pounded until fluffy
- 50g pork rinds (tóp mỡ), fried crispy
- Scallion oil (mỡ hành)
- Nước chấm for dipping
instructions:
1. Grease small saucers or ramekins.
