2. Ladle a thin layer of batter into each saucer.
3. Steam over boiling water for 8–10 minutes until set and glossy on top.
4. Remove from steamer. Top each cake with dried shrimp floss, a piece of pork rind, and a spoonful of scallion oil.
5. Serve with nước chấm on the side for dipping.
tips: The batter layer should be thin — you want a delicate, slightly translucent cake. Too thick and it becomes chewy rather than silky.

name: Bánh Khọt
description: Crispy mini rice pancakes made in a special cast-iron mold, filled with shrimp and coconut milk batter — crunchy on the outside, custardy in the center. A Southern specialty from Vũng Tàu, eaten wrapped in lettuce with herbs and nước chấm.
tagline: Mini crispy pancakes from Vũng Tàu's street corners
ingredients:
- For batter: 200g rice flour, 1 tbsp tapioca starch, 200ml coconut milk, 200ml water, ½ tsp turmeric, ½ tsp salt
- 200g small shrimp, peeled
- 3 scallions, sliced
- Coconut oil or neutral oil for the mold
- To serve: lettuce, mint, perilla, nước chấm
instructions:
1. Whisk batter ingredients until smooth. Rest 20 minutes.
2. Heat bánh khọt mold (or takoyaki pan) over medium heat. Brush cavities generously with oil.
3. Fill each cavity halfway with batter. Place 1–2 shrimp in each. Top with a little scallion.
4. Cover and cook 3–4 minutes until edges are set and bottom is crispy.
5. Loosen with a toothpick and flip to crisp the other side briefly, 1 minute.
6. Serve hot with fresh herbs and nước chấm.
tips: The mold must be very hot and well-oiled before adding batter — this is what creates the crispy shell rather than a stuck, soggy pancake.

name: Bánh Bột Lọc
description: Translucent shrimp and pork dumplings from Huế — made with tapioca starch dough that becomes completely see-through when cooked, revealing the filling inside. Eaten with nước chấm, one at a time, in a state of near reverence.
tagline: Tapioca dumplings, translucent and chewy
ingredients:
- For dough: 200g tapioca starch + 50ml boiling water, mixed until a smooth, non-sticky dough forms (add water slowly)
- For filling: 150g shrimp, peeled and roughly chopped + 100g pork belly, finely diced + 1 tbsp fish sauce + 1 tsp sugar + white pepper
- Banana leaf or parchment paper for wrapping
- Nước chấm for serving
instructions:
1. Cook filling: stir-fry pork belly until rendered, add shrimp and season with fish sauce, sugar, pepper. Cool.
2. Divide dough into small pieces. Flatten each into a thin round on a greased surface.
3. Place 1 tsp filling in center. Fold over and seal edges tightly, pressing firmly.
4. Boil a large pot of water. Cook dumplings for 7–8 minutes until translucent and floating.
5. Remove with a slotted spoon. Serve with nước chấm.
tips: The dough dries out quickly — work with small portions and cover unused dough with plastic wrap. The water must be boiling hard or the dumplings will fall apart.

name: Gỏi Ngó Sen
description: Lotus stem salad with shrimp, pork, fresh herbs, and a bright lime-fish sauce dressing. The lotus stems are crunchy and slightly hollow — they hold the dressing and crunch against the tender shrimp and pork in a way that is deeply satisfying.
tagline: Lotus stem salad, light and elegant
ingredients:
- 300g fresh lotus stems, peeled and sliced diagonally (or canned, rinsed)
- 200g shrimp, peeled and cooked
- 150g pork belly, boiled and thinly sliced
- Small handful Vietnamese coriander (rau răm)
- Fried shallots, crushed peanuts
- For dressing: 3 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 garlic clove minced, 1 chili sliced
instructions:
1. If using fresh lotus stems: soak in water with a squeeze of lime for 10 minutes to prevent browning. Drain.
2. Blanch lotus stems in boiling water for 1–2 minutes until just tender-crisp. Drain and cool.
3. Mix dressing ingredients.
4. Toss lotus stems, shrimp, pork, and rau răm with dressing.
5. Taste and adjust.
6. Plate and top with fried shallots and peanuts.
tips: Don't overcook the lotus stems — the crunch is the entire point. 1–2 minutes maximum in boiling water.

name: Gỏi Bưởi Tôm Thịt
description: Pomelo salad with shrimp and pork — the citrus segments are separated into fluffy, slightly bitter individual sacs that hold dressing without going soggy. Combined with fresh herbs and a sweet-sour fish sauce dressing, it is one of the most refreshing Vietnamese salads.
tagline: Pomelo salad with shrimp — refreshing and bright
ingredients:
- 1 pomelo, peeled, segments broken into individual sacs by hand
- 200g shrimp, cooked and peeled
- 150g pork belly, boiled and sliced
- Fresh mint and Vietnamese coriander
- Fried shallots, crushed peanuts
- For dressing: 2 tbsp fish sauce, 2 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 chili sliced
instructions:
1. Break pomelo segments apart into fluffy individual sacs. Remove any seeds or tough membrane.
2. Make dressing, adjusting sugar and lime to balance the pomelo's natural bitterness.
3. Toss pomelo, shrimp, pork, and fresh herbs gently in dressing.
4. Top with fried shallots and peanuts.
tips: The pomelo sacs should be feathery and separate, not crushed. Use your fingers to pull apart, not a knife — breaking them releases too much juice.

name: Phá Lấu
description: A Sài Gòn street snack of braised offal — pig ears, intestines, tofu, and quail eggs simmered in a spiced coconut-based broth with five spice and star anise. Sold from carts in little plastic bags with extra broth. More approachable than it sounds.
tagline: Offal braised in spiced coconut water — a Sài Gòn late-night classic
ingredients:
- 300g pig ears, cleaned and cut into strips
- 200g pig intestines, cleaned and cut into pieces
- 8 quail eggs, boiled and peeled
- 200g fried tofu puffs
- 200ml coconut water
- 3 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar
- 3 star anise, 1 cinnamon stick, 3 cloves
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- 3 shallots, sliced
- Chili sauce and bánh mì for serving
instructions:
1. Blanch pig ears and intestines in boiling water for 10 minutes. Drain.
2. Heat oil in a pot. Fry shallots until golden. Add spices, fry 30 seconds.
3. Add all offal, coconut water, soy sauce, fish sauce, and sugar. Bring to a boil.
4. Reduce to low simmer. Add quail eggs and tofu puffs. Cook 45–50 minutes until tender.
5. Taste and adjust seasoning.
6. Serve in small bowls with extra braising liquid, chili sauce, and torn bánh mì.
tips: Cleaning the offal properly (blanching, scrubbing) is the only difficult part. Once that's done, the braise is forgiving.

name: Chè Bắp
description: Sweet corn dessert soup — fresh corn simmered with coconut milk and pandan until the kernels are tender and the liquid turns milky and fragrant. Simple, seasonal, and restorative. Eaten warm in the evening or cool in the afternoon.
tagline: Sweet corn pudding — summer in a bowl
ingredients:
- 3 ears fresh sweet corn, kernels cut off cobs (keep cobs)
- 400ml coconut milk
- 400ml water
- 3 tbsp sugar, or to taste
- 2 pandan leaves, tied in a knot
- Pinch of salt
- Salted coconut cream for topping (as in chè đậu đỏ)
instructions:
1. Simmer corn cobs in water with pandan leaves for 15 minutes to extract a sweet corn stock. Remove cobs.
2. Add corn kernels to the cob stock. Cook 8–10 minutes until tender.
3. Add coconut milk, sugar, and salt. Simmer 5 more minutes.
4. Taste — adjust sweetness. The soup should be gently sweet and fragrant.
5. Serve warm or at room temperature, topped with a spoonful of salted coconut cream.
tips: Simmering the cobs first pulls corn flavor into the liquid and makes a dramatically more flavorful base than starting with water alone.

name: Chè Trôi Nước
description: Glutinous rice dumplings filled with sweetened mung bean paste, floating in a warm ginger syrup with coconut cream drizzled on top. The dumplings bob and gleam — trôi nước means 'floating in water.' A Southern Vietnamese dessert that is also eaten at the winter solstice festival.
tagline: Floating rice dumplings in ginger syrup
ingredients:
- For dumplings: 200g glutinous rice flour + 120–130ml warm water — knead to a smooth dough
- For filling: 100g split mung beans, cooked until soft, mashed with 2 tbsp sugar and a drop of vanilla
- For ginger syrup: 600ml water + 80g sugar + 5cm fresh ginger, sliced and bruised
- Salted coconut cream, sesame seeds to finish
instructions:
1. Simmer ginger syrup ingredients for 15 minutes. Strain out ginger. Keep warm.
2. Portion mung bean filling into small balls, about 1 tsp each. Freeze briefly to firm.
3. Take a small piece of dough, flatten, wrap around a mung bean ball, seal and roll into a smooth sphere.
4. Boil a large pot of water. Cook dumplings until they float, then 2 more minutes. Remove.
5. Place in bowls of warm ginger syrup.
6. Drizzle salted coconut cream and scatter sesame seeds over.
tips: The dough should be soft and pliable but not sticky. Too dry and it cracks; too wet and it won't seal. Test one dumpling first.

name: Chè Đậu Xanh
description: Split mung bean sweet soup — the milder, softer sibling of chè đậu đỏ. The mung beans cook into a creamy, almost velvety texture when split, and the pandan and coconut cream make it fragrant and rich. One of Vietnam's most everyday desserts.
tagline: Mung bean sweet soup, cool and comforting
ingredients:
- 250g split mung beans (đậu xanh đã bỏ vỏ), soaked 1 hour
- 800ml water
- 60g sugar, or to taste
- 2 pandan leaves, tied
- Pinch of salt
- Salted coconut cream for topping
instructions:
1. Drain soaked mung beans. Add to pot with water and pandan leaves.
2. Bring to boil, simmer 25–30 minutes until completely soft and some beans have broken down into a creamy broth.
3. Add sugar and salt. Simmer 5 more minutes.
4. Remove pandan. Serve warm or at room temperature with salted coconut cream drizzled over.
tips: Split mung beans cook much faster than whole. Stir occasionally in the last 10 minutes as the thick beans are prone to catching on the bottom.

name: Bánh Da Lợn
description: Steamed layered rice cake with alternating translucent tapioca layers and opaque pandan or mung bean layers. Each layer is set before the next is poured — the result is a striped, jewel-like cake with a satisfyingly bouncy, jelly-like texture.
tagline: Layered taro cake, sliced and steamed
ingredients:
- For tapioca layer: 100g tapioca starch + 150ml coconut milk + 100ml water + 80g sugar + pinch of salt
- For pandan layer: 100g tapioca starch + 50g rice flour + 150ml coconut milk + 100ml pandan juice (blend 8 pandan leaves with water, strain) + 60g sugar
- For mung bean layer (optional): 150g cooked mashed mung beans mixed into second batter
instructions:
1. Mix each batter separately until very smooth. Strain.
2. Grease a round 20cm cake pan. Place in a steamer.
3. Pour a thin layer (about 80ml) of the first batter into the pan. Steam 5–6 minutes until just set.
4. Pour an equal layer of the second batter on top. Steam 5–6 minutes.
5. Alternate layers until all batter is used, steaming each layer fully before adding the next.
6. Let cool completely before cutting. Slice with an oiled knife.
tips: Each layer must be fully set before pouring the next or they will bleed into each other and lose the distinct stripes. Be patient between layers.

name: Bánh Trôi
description: Smooth glutinous rice balls filled with raw sugar (đường phên) or sweetened mung bean, boiled until they float and served in a pandan-scented syrup or plain. The Tết Hàn Thực dish — made on the Cold Food Festival in the third lunar month.
tagline: Sesame rice balls in sweet ginger broth
ingredients:
- 200g glutinous rice flour
- 120ml warm water
- 80g raw brown sugar (đường phên) or sweetened mung bean paste, portioned into small cubes
- Sesame seeds, toasted
- Shredded coconut (optional)
instructions:
1. Knead glutinous rice flour with warm water until smooth and pliable.
2. Divide into walnut-sized pieces. Flatten each, place a cube of sugar or bean paste in the center, seal tightly and roll smooth.
3. Boil in a large pot of water until balls float and skins are translucent, about 5–7 minutes.
4. Remove with a slotted spoon. Roll in toasted sesame seeds and coconut if using.
5. Serve as is, or in a light pandan syrup.
tips: Seal the filling completely — any gap will cause the sugar to leak into the water during cooking and you'll lose the sweet center.

name: Chè Thái
description: A Vietnamese-Thai inspired layered dessert in a glass — coconut milk over shaved ice with jackfruit, lychee, grass jelly, tapioca pearls, and palm seeds. Extravagant in ingredients, easy to assemble, and deeply refreshing in the heat.
tagline: Thai-style chè with coconut milk and jackfruit
ingredients:
- 200ml coconut milk + 2 tbsp sugar + pinch of salt, warmed and stirred
- 100g tapioca pearls, cooked until translucent, drained
- 100g grass jelly (sương sáo), cubed
- 100g canned jackfruit, sliced
- 100g canned lychee or longan, drained
- 50g palm seeds (hạt lựu) or water chestnuts
- Shaved or crushed ice
instructions:
1. Cook tapioca pearls per package until fully translucent. Drain and toss with a little sugar syrup to prevent sticking.
2. Prepare all toppings.
3. In each glass: layer grass jelly, jackfruit, lychee, tapioca pearls, and palm seeds.
4. Pack shaved ice over the top.
5. Pour sweetened coconut milk over everything.
6. Serve immediately with a long spoon.
tips: Prepare and chill all components before assembling. Everything should be cold when it meets the ice or it will melt too quickly.

name: Kem Dừa
description: Vietnamese coconut ice cream — dense, creamy, and aggressively coconut-forward, served inside a young coconut shell with toasted peanuts, coconut flesh strips, and rainbow jelly. The coconut shell is not decoration; eating the soft shell with the ice cream is the move.
tagline: Coconut ice cream in a coconut shell
ingredients:
- 400ml full-fat coconut milk
- 100ml coconut cream
- 80g sugar
- 2 tbsp coconut oil
- Pinch of salt
- 1 tsp vanilla
- To serve: toasted peanuts, strips of young coconut flesh, colored tapioca jelly
instructions:
1. Warm coconut milk, coconut cream, sugar, coconut oil, and salt in a saucepan until sugar dissolves. Don't boil.
2. Cool completely, then add vanilla.
3. Churn in an ice cream machine according to manufacturer instructions, or pour into a freezer container and stir every 30 minutes for 3 hours.
4. Serve scooped into a young coconut shell or bowl.
5. Top with peanuts, coconut flesh strips, and jelly.
tips: No-churn version: after cooling, whip the mixture lightly before freezing. Stir every 30 minutes for the first 3 hours to break up ice crystals.

name: Xôi Lá Dứa
description: Sticky rice colored and perfumed with fresh pandan juice — vibrant green, fragrant, and slightly sweet. Served with coconut cream and toasted sesame or mung bean crumble. Both dessert and breakfast, eaten from a small banana leaf.
tagline: Green rice scented with pandan
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- Pandan juice: blend 8–10 pandan leaves with 200ml water, strain — deep green liquid
- 1 tsp salt
- Salted coconut cream topping
- Toasted sesame seeds or mung bean crumble
instructions:
1. Drain soaked rice. Toss with pandan juice (use enough to coat well and give a deep green color) and salt.
2. Steam over boiling water for 35–40 minutes, fluffing halfway, until cooked through and fragrant.
3. The rice should be uniformly green and sticky.
4. Serve warm, drizzled with salted coconut cream and scattered with sesame seeds.
tips: Fresh pandan juice gives a far more vibrant green and fragrant result than extract. Blend, strain, and use immediately — it loses color quickly.

name: Xôi Gấc
description: Red sticky rice made with gấc fruit — a spiky Southeast Asian fruit whose deep red-orange flesh produces a stunning crimson color in the rice, along with a subtle sweetness. Made for Tết and wedding celebrations, it is the most festive dish in Vietnamese cooking.
tagline: Red sticky rice for Tết and every celebration
ingredients:
- 400g glutinous rice, soaked overnight
- 3 tbsp gấc fruit flesh (from frozen gấc, or substitute 2 tbsp annatto oil for color, though flavor differs)
- 2 tbsp sugar
- 1 tbsp neutral oil
- 1 tsp salt
- Coconut milk (optional)
instructions:
1. Drain soaked rice. Mix with gấc flesh, sugar, oil, and salt until the rice is evenly coated and deep red.
2. Steam over boiling water for 35–40 minutes, fluffing halfway through.
3. The rice should be a uniform deep crimson and completely sticky.
4. Serve in a mound, often shaped in a round bowl and turned out. Drizzle with coconut milk if desired.
tips: Wear gloves when handling gấc — the natural dye stains everything permanently. The color fades during cooking so start with more gấc than you think you need.

name: Bánh Bao
description: Vietnamese steamed buns — pillowy, slightly sweet white dough filled with a pork and egg filling. Sold from glass cases at every Vietnamese bakery and street corner. The soft-as-cloud exterior and dense, savory filling is a perfect portable meal.
tagline: Fluffy steamed bun, stuffed with pork and egg
ingredients:
- For dough: 300g all-purpose flour, 1 tsp instant yeast, 1 tbsp sugar, ½ tsp salt, ½ tsp baking powder, 160ml warm milk, 1 tbsp neutral oil
- For filling: 300g ground pork, 1 hard-boiled egg (quartered), 30g glass noodles (soaked, chopped), 20g wood ear mushroom (soaked, minced), 2 tbsp oyster sauce, 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar, white pepper
instructions:
1. Mix dough ingredients, knead 8–10 minutes until smooth. Rest 1 hour until doubled.
2. Mix filling ingredients well. Divide into 8 portions. Wrap a piece of egg in each portion.
3. Punch down dough, divide into 8 pieces. Roll each into a round about 12cm.
4. Place filling in center, gather edges, pinch and twist to seal tightly.
5. Place on squares of parchment paper. Rest 15 minutes.
6. Steam over high heat for 15–18 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: The dough should be slightly sweet and soft. If the bun deflates after steaming, the filling was too wet or the bun was opened too soon. Let rest 2 minutes before opening the steamer.

name: Nem Chua
description: Fermented pork roll — cured, slightly sour, pleasantly funky slices of ground pork mixed with shredded pork skin and garlic, wrapped in banana leaves and left to ferment for 3–5 days. A Southern snack eaten raw with fresh chili and garlic.
tagline: Fermented pork roll — sour, funky, beloved
ingredients:
- 500g pork loin, ground fine
- 150g pork skin, boiled until tender and shredded into thin strips
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 1 tsp white pepper
- 1 tsp saltpeter (muối diêm, optional but traditional)
- 5 cloves garlic, thinly sliced
- Fresh chili slices
- Banana leaves for wrapping
instructions:
1. Mix ground pork with pork skin, fish sauce, sugar, pepper, and saltpeter until very sticky.
2. Portion into small cylinders (about 50g each).
3. Lay garlic and chili slices on a banana leaf. Place pork on top, roll tightly. Wrap in another leaf layer and secure with string or rubber bands.
4. Leave at room temperature (25–30°C) for 3–5 days. Taste from day 3.
5. Ready when the surface feels slightly tacky and the flavor is pleasantly sour and tangy.
6. Eat as is, with extra garlic and chili.
tips: Nem chua is eaten raw after fermentation — the acidity from the natural fermentation process cures the meat safely. In warm climates, 3 days is usually enough.

name: Bánh Giò
description: A pyramid-shaped dumpling from Northern Vietnam — tapioca and rice flour dough wrapped around a pork and mushroom filling, then folded in banana leaf and steamed until the exterior becomes smooth, slightly slippery, and entirely white. Unwrapped at the table.
tagline: Pyramid-shaped steamed cake, soft and savory
ingredients:
- For dough: 200g rice flour, 50g tapioca starch, 500ml water, 1 tbsp neutral oil, 1 tsp salt — cook over medium heat, stirring constantly until thick and translucent
- For filling: 200g ground pork, 30g wood ear mushrooms (soaked, minced), 2 shallots (minced), 1 tbsp fish sauce, white pepper
- Banana leaves, cut into 25cm squares, softened over flame
instructions:
1. Cook dough: combine flour, starch, water, oil, and salt in a pan. Stir constantly over medium heat until it becomes a thick, translucent paste that pulls away from the sides. Cool.
2. Cook filling: stir-fry shallots, add pork and mushrooms. Season with fish sauce and pepper. Cool.
3. Lay banana leaf. Spoon a portion of dough, flatten. Add filling in center. Fold dough around filling.
4. Fold banana leaf into a pyramid shape and secure.
5. Steam for 25–30 minutes. Serve in the leaf.
tips: The dough cooking step is the key technique — it must be cooked thoroughly or it will be gummy and fall apart when steamed.

name: Bạc Xỉu
description: A Sài Gòn original — more milk than coffee, sweetened with condensed milk and served over ice. Where cà phê sữa đá is coffee with milk, bạc xỉu is milk with coffee. The name is Cantonese-Vietnamese slang for 'a little dark.' Light, sweet, and perfect for those who find regular Vietnamese coffee too intense.
tagline: Milky coffee, sweeter and lighter than cà phê đen
ingredients:
- 1 shot strong Vietnamese coffee (from a phin, or espresso)
- 3–4 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 100ml warm whole milk
- Ice
instructions:
1. Add condensed milk to a glass.
2. Brew coffee into a small amount and let cool slightly.
3. Fill glass with ice.
4. Pour warm milk over ice.
5. Add coffee last — pour slowly so it sits on top briefly before being stirred.
6. Stir and drink.
tips: The ratio is reversed from cà phê sữa đá — this should taste primarily of sweet milk with coffee as a note, not the other way around.

name: Sữa Đậu Nành
description: Homemade soy milk, warm and fragrant with pandan — a staple of Vietnamese morning street carts alongside bánh mì and xôi. Light, naturally sweet, and deeply comforting. Entirely different from store-bought soy milk.
tagline: Fresh soy milk, warm or cold
ingredients:
- 200g dried soybeans, soaked overnight in plenty of water
- 1.2L water
- 2 pandan leaves, tied
- 2–3 tbsp sugar, to taste
- Pinch of salt
instructions:
1. Drain and rinse soaked soybeans. Blend with 600ml fresh water in batches until very smooth.
2. Strain through a muslin cloth or fine mesh, squeezing to extract all milk. Discard pulp.
3. Pour soy milk into a pot with remaining water, pandan leaves, sugar, and salt.
4. Bring to a boil over medium heat, stirring, then reduce to a simmer for 10 minutes.
5. Remove pandan. Taste and adjust sweetness.
6. Serve warm or over ice.
tips: Must cook the raw soy milk thoroughly — raw soy contains compounds that can cause digestive upset. A full 10-minute simmer after it comes to a boil is essential.

name: Nước Mía
description: Fresh sugarcane juice pressed to order through a hand-cranked or electric press, served over ice with a squeeze of kumquat — one of the most refreshing drinks in Vietnam. The juice is grassy, sweet, faintly earthy, and nothing like any juice from a bottle.
tagline: Fresh sugarcane juice, crushed at the cart
ingredients:
- 2–3 stalks fresh sugarcane, peeled and cut into 15cm lengths
- 2–3 small kumquats or ½ lime
- Ice
instructions:
1. Feed sugarcane through a sugarcane juicer or heavy-duty juicer, pressing multiple times for maximum yield.
2. Pour juice over a glass of ice.
3. Squeeze a kumquat or lime over the top.
4. Stir and drink immediately — the juice oxidizes quickly.
tips: Drink within minutes of pressing — sugarcane juice oxidizes and loses its brightness rapidly. This is why it's always made fresh and consumed immediately.

name: Rau Má
description: Pennywort juice — blended fresh pennywort leaves with honey and a little lime, strained to a thin, vibrant green drink. Cooling and slightly bitter, consumed in Vietnamese food culture for its perceived health benefits. Drunk cold in the summer heat.
tagline: Pennywort juice — grassy, cool, good for you
ingredients:
- 100g fresh pennywort leaves (rau má), washed
- 400ml cold water
- 1 tbsp honey or sugar, to taste
- Juice of ½ lime
- Ice
instructions:
1. Blend pennywort leaves with cold water until very smooth.
2. Strain through a fine mesh to remove fibrous bits.
3. Add honey and lime juice. Stir well.
4. Taste and adjust sweetness.
5. Serve over ice immediately.
tips: Drink fresh — pennywort juice turns brown and bitter if left too long. Blend and serve within 15 minutes.

name: Trà Đào
description: Peach iced tea — Vietnamese-style with house-made peach syrup, cold-brewed jasmine tea, and a heap of ice. The style popularized by milk tea shops across Vietnam: sweet, fruity, and deeply refreshing. Simple to make at home and better than any chain version.
tagline: Peach iced tea — sweet, floral, everywhere in Hà Nội
ingredients:
- For peach syrup: 2 ripe peaches (or 1 can canned peaches) + 80g sugar + 100ml water — simmer 10 minutes, blend, strain
- 400ml cold-brewed jasmine tea (steep 2 tbsp jasmine tea in 400ml cold water for 8 hours)
- Ice
- Fresh peach slices and mint to serve
instructions:
1. Make peach syrup: simmer peaches, sugar, and water until fruit is soft. Blend smooth and strain. Cool.
2. Cold-brew jasmine tea overnight in the fridge.
3. Fill glasses with ice. Add 3–4 tbsp peach syrup per glass.
4. Pour cold jasmine tea over.
5. Stir, garnish with peach slices and mint.
tips: Cold-brewed tea is less bitter and more delicate than hot-brewed and cooled. The overnight steep is worth planning for.

name: Nước Sâm
description: A cooling herbal drink brewed from a combination of dried herbs — including imperata root, coix seeds, and dried sugarcane — believed in Vietnamese tradition to reduce body heat. Lightly sweet, slightly medicinal, and completely refreshing over ice.
tagline: Herbal drink, slow-simmered, served cold in summer
ingredients:
- 30g dried mía lau (dried sugarcane pieces)
- 20g bạch mao căn (imperata root)
- 20g ý dĩ (coix/job's tears)
- 1.2L water
- Rock sugar to taste
- 2 pandan leaves (optional)
instructions:
1. Rinse all dried herbs under cold water.
2. Combine herbs and water in a pot. Bring to a boil.
3. Reduce heat and simmer for 25–30 minutes.
4. Add rock sugar and pandan leaves if using. Simmer 5 more minutes.
5. Strain into a jug. Cool completely.
6. Serve over ice.
tips: Nước sâm mix is sold pre-packaged at Vietnamese grocery stores — look for herbal cooling tea blends if you can't source individual ingredients.

name: Sinh Tố Dâu
description: Vietnamese strawberry smoothie — blended with condensed milk, whole milk, and ice until thick and bright pink. Richer than a Western smoothie, sweeter than a milkshake, and sold from every trái cây sinh tố cart in Vietnam.
tagline: Strawberry smoothie, bright and sweet
ingredients:
- 150g fresh strawberries, hulled
- 2 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 80ml whole milk
- 8–10 ice cubes
instructions:
1. Add all ingredients to a blender.
2. Blend on high until completely smooth and thick.
3. Taste — add more condensed milk if needed.
4. Pour into a glass and serve immediately.
tips: Vietnamese sinh tố should be thick enough to barely pour — if it's thin and watery, add more fruit or less milk. The condensed milk is the sweetener; no extra sugar needed.

name: Sinh Tố Xoài
description: Mango smoothie blended Vietnamese-style — with condensed milk and ice until thick and golden. Uses ripe, fragrant Vietnamese mango varieties like cát Hòa Lộc or Thái mango. Eaten as much as drunk.
tagline: Mango smoothie — tropical and thick
ingredients:
- 1 large ripe mango, peeled and diced (about 250g)
- 2 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 60ml whole milk
- 8 ice cubes
instructions:
1. Blend mango, condensed milk, milk, and ice until thick and smooth.
2. Taste — ripe mango may need less condensed milk.
3. Pour into a glass. Serve immediately.
tips: The mango must be ripe to the point of being soft and deeply fragrant. Underripe mango makes a bland, stringy smoothie no amount of condensed milk can fix.

name: Trà Gừng Mật Ong
description: Ginger and honey tea — strong fresh ginger sliced and steeped in hot water with honey and a squeeze of lime. A winter warming drink in Northern Vietnam, drunk when cold or under the weather. Aggressively gingery, sweet, and clarifying.
tagline: Ginger honey tea for cold mornings
ingredients:
- 5cm fresh ginger, peeled and thinly sliced
- 400ml water
- 2 tbsp honey, or to taste
- Juice of ½ lime
- Optional: 1 lemongrass stalk, bruised
instructions:
1. Bring water to a boil with ginger (and lemongrass if using). Simmer 8–10 minutes.
2. Remove from heat. Add honey and lime juice.
3. Stir until honey dissolves.
4. Strain into cups.
5. Drink hot.
tips: Add honey off the heat to preserve its flavor compounds. Boiling honey dulls it. The tea should be assertively spicy from the ginger — adjust quantity to taste.

name: Cà Phê Dừa
description: Coconut coffee — a Hội An specialty that has swept Vietnam. Strong cold-brew or iced Vietnamese coffee blended with coconut cream and ice until thick and frothy, poured over more ice. Somewhere between a coffee and a coconut smoothie.
tagline: Iced coffee blended with coconut milk
ingredients:
- 1 shot strong Vietnamese coffee, cooled
- 100ml coconut cream
- 1 tbsp sweetened condensed milk (or coconut sugar)
- 8 ice cubes
- Extra ice for serving
instructions:
1. Brew coffee and cool to room temperature.
2. Blend cooled coffee, coconut cream, condensed milk, and ice until thick and frothy.
3. Pour over a glass of fresh ice.
4. Drink immediately.
tips: Use full-fat coconut cream, not coconut milk — the thickness is what gives this its distinctive creamy texture. Coconut milk will make it watery.

name: Trà Sữa Trân Châu
description: Vietnamese bubble tea — jasmine or oolong tea mixed with condensed milk and tapioca pearls. The Vietnamese version tends to be less sweet than Taiwanese, with a stronger tea flavor and the signature Vietnamese condensed milk richness.
tagline: Milk tea with tapioca pearls — a Việt Nam institution
ingredients:
- 100g black tapioca pearls
- 400ml strong-brewed jasmine or oolong tea, cooled
- 4 tbsp sweetened condensed milk
- 100ml whole milk
- Ice
- Brown sugar syrup for pearls: 3 tbsp brown sugar + 2 tbsp water, simmered to a light syrup
instructions:
1. Cook tapioca pearls per package (usually 15–20 minutes until fully cooked through with no white center). Drain and toss in brown sugar syrup while warm.
2. Brew tea, cool completely.
3. Mix cooled tea with condensed milk and whole milk. Stir well.
4. Add ice to glasses. Spoon pearls in first.
5. Pour tea mixture over.
6. Serve with a fat straw.
tips: Tapioca pearls are best eaten within 2–4 hours of cooking — they harden in the fridge. Make fresh each time.

name: Hủ Tiếu Xào
description: Stir-fried rice noodles with pork, shrimp, bean sprouts, and chives — the drier, wok-fried version of hủ tiếu that's cooked over high heat until the noodles get some char at the edges. A quick, satisfying noodle dish that showcases the wok.
tagline: Stir-fried noodles, fast and satisfying
ingredients:
- 300g fresh rice noodles (hủ tiếu tươi) or flat rice noodles, softened
- 150g pork shoulder, thinly sliced
- 150g shrimp, peeled
- 100g bean sprouts
- Handful garlic chives or scallions, cut into 3cm lengths
- 3 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp oyster sauce
- 1 tbsp soy sauce
- 1 tsp sesame oil
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
instructions:
1. Heat wok over highest heat until smoking. Add oil.
2. Stir-fry garlic 20 seconds. Add pork slices, cook until just done.
3. Add shrimp, cook 1 minute.
4. Add noodles. Toss and spread across wok surface — let sit 30–45 seconds without touching to get char.
5. Add oyster sauce, soy sauce. Toss vigorously.
6. Add bean sprouts and chives in the last minute. Toss once.
7. Finish with sesame oil. Serve immediately.
tips: Patience is key — let the noodles sit against the hot wok surface to char before tossing. Constant stirring prevents the char and kills the wok hei.

name: Cháo Lòng
description: Offal congee — silky pork rice porridge topped with sliced pork intestines, heart, liver, and blood pudding. A deeply savory, rich breakfast for people who know what they like. Eaten from carts in Sài Gòn alongside quẩy (Chinese fried dough) for dipping.
tagline: Offal congee — adventurous, warming, deeply satisfying
ingredients:
- 200g jasmine rice
- 1.5L pork stock
- 150g pork intestines, cleaned and cut into rings
- 100g pork heart, sliced
- 100g pork liver, thinly sliced
- 50g blood pudding (tiết canh set), sliced (optional)
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, salt, white pepper
- Ginger, 3cm, bruised
- Fried shallots, sliced scallions, fresh ginger julienned, cilantro, lime to serve
instructions:
1. Simmer rice in pork stock with ginger over medium-low heat for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally, until porridge is thick and smooth.
2. Blanch intestines and heart in boiling salted water for 5 minutes. Drain.
3. Season porridge with fish sauce, salt, and white pepper.
4. In individual bowls: ladle porridge, top with intestines, heart, and a few slices of raw liver (which cooks in the hot porridge).
5. Add blood pudding if using, fried shallots, scallions, and ginger.
6. Serve with quẩy, lime, and extra chili.
tips: Thorough cleaning of the intestines is essential — turn inside out, rub with salt and cornstarch, rinse repeatedly. The blanching step removes any residual odor.

name: Bánh Canh Chả Cá
description: Thick noodle soup with fish cakes — a Central Vietnamese specialty from Nha Trang and Quy Nhơn. Golden-fried fish cakes float in a clear, sweet fish stock alongside thick tapioca noodles, eaten with lime and chili.
tagline: Thick noodles with fish cake — a coastal staple
ingredients:
- For fish cakes: 400g firm white fish fillet (mackerel or Spanish mackerel), minced + 1 tbsp fish sauce + 1 tsp sugar + white pepper + 2 tbsp neutral oil — mix until sticky, form patties
- 500g fresh bánh canh noodles
- 1L fish or pork stock
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar
- Lemongrass and shallots for stock aromatics
- Scallions, fried shallots, lime, chili
instructions:
1. Mix fish cake ingredients vigorously until sticky. Shape into patties or balls.
2. Pan-fry fish cakes in oil until golden on both sides. Set aside.
3. Simmer stock with lemongrass and shallots for 20 minutes. Strain and season.
4. Cook bánh canh noodles in the simmering broth for 5–7 minutes until translucent.
5. Divide noodles into bowls. Add fish cakes and ladle broth over.
6. Top with scallions and fried shallots. Serve with lime and chili.
tips: Mix the fish paste very well — the more you mix, the bouncier and more firm the fish cakes become. Undermixed fish cakes will be crumbly.

name: Lươn Xào Sả Ớt
description: Eel stir-fried with lemongrass and chili — a Northern Vietnamese specialty found in Hà Nội's eel noodle restaurants and home kitchens. The eel is meaty, slightly sweet, and takes lemongrass and chili exceptionally well. Served over rice or noodles.
tagline: Spicy eel stir-fry — bold, fiery, addictive
ingredients:
- 400g freshwater eel, cleaned and cut into 4cm pieces
- 3 stalks lemongrass, white parts minced
- 3 dried red chilies or 1 fresh chili, sliced
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 2 tbsp fish sauce
- 1 tsp sugar
- 1 tbsp turmeric powder
- 2 tbsp neutral oil
- Fresh dill and scallions to finish
instructions:
1. Toss eel pieces with turmeric, a pinch of salt, and 1 tbsp fish sauce. Marinate 15 minutes.
2. Heat oil in a wok or pan over high heat. Add garlic and lemongrass, stir-fry until fragrant and golden.
3. Add eel, spreading in a single layer. Cook without moving for 2 minutes to form a crust.
4. Toss and continue cooking 5–6 minutes until cooked through and slightly caramelized.
5. Add remaining fish sauce, sugar, and chili. Toss to combine.
6. Finish with dill and scallions. Serve over rice or rice noodles.
tips: Fresh or live eel gives the best texture. Frozen eel works but releases more water during cooking — pat very dry before marinating.

name: Hàu Nướng Mỡ Hành
description: Grilled oysters with scallion oil and crushed peanuts — a coastal Vietnamese staple from Nha Trang and Phú Quốc. Live oysters grilled over charcoal until the edges curl and bubble, then doused in sizzling scallion oil and topped with peanuts.
tagline: Grilled oysters with scallion oil — seaside luxury
ingredients:
- 12 fresh oysters, on the half shell
- Scallion oil (mỡ hành) — 4 scallion stalks sliced + 3 tbsp oil heated until smoking
- 3 tbsp crushed roasted peanuts
- 1 tbsp fried shallots
- Sriracha or chili sauce
- Lime wedges
instructions:
1. Prepare scallion oil: heat oil until smoking, pour over sliced scallions. It will sizzle. Set aside.
2. Place oysters on a grill over high charcoal heat (or in a very hot oven).
3. Grill until the oyster juices bubble and the edges just begin to curl, 4–6 minutes.
4. Remove carefully. Spoon scallion oil over each oyster immediately.
5. Top with crushed peanuts, fried shallots, and a few drops of sriracha.
6. Squeeze lime over. Eat in one move.
tips: Don't overcook — oysters should be just set, not rubbery. The moment the edges begin to curl is the moment to remove them.

name: Gỏi Sứa
description: Jellyfish salad — translucent strips of jellyfish dressed with sesame oil, chili, and fish sauce, tossed with pork, shrimp, herbs, and peanuts. The jellyfish is firm and crunchy in a way that no other ingredient can replicate. A beloved Southern snack that rewards the adventurous.
tagline: Jellyfish salad — chewy, cool, an acquired joy
ingredients:
- 200g prepared jellyfish strips (packed in salt, soaked and rinsed thoroughly)
- 150g cooked shrimp, peeled
- 100g pork belly, boiled and sliced
- 1 carrot, julienned
- Fresh Vietnamese coriander (rau răm) and mint
- 1 tbsp sesame oil
- For dressing: 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp lime juice, 1 tbsp sugar, 1 garlic clove minced, 1 chili sliced
- Crushed peanuts, fried shallots
instructions:
1. Soak packaged jellyfish in cold water for 30 minutes, changing water twice to remove salt. Drain well.
2. Blanch jellyfish briefly in hot (not boiling) water for 30 seconds — this firms the texture. Drain and cool.
3. Toss with sesame oil.
4. Mix dressing ingredients.
5. Combine jellyfish, shrimp, pork, carrot, and herbs. Pour dressing over and toss.
6. Top with peanuts and fried shallots.
tips: Don't over-blanch the jellyfish — it should be firm and slightly crunchy, not soft. Hot water for 30 seconds maximum.

name: Bún Kèn
description: Phú Quốc's famous fish and coconut noodle soup — fish poached in a coconut milk broth fragrant with lemongrass, turmeric, and galangal, poured over rice vermicelli with a pile of fresh herbs and crunchy accompaniments. Little known outside the island.
tagline: Coconut milk broth, steamed fish, Phú Quốc's pride
ingredients:
- 400g firm white fish (Spanish mackerel or tuna), poached and flaked
- 400ml coconut milk
- 300ml fish or pork stock
- 2 stalks lemongrass, bruised
- 2cm galangal, sliced
- 1 tsp turmeric powder
- 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tsp sugar
- 400g rice vermicelli, cooked
- To serve: bean sprouts, shredded banana blossom, cucumber, fresh mint, lime, sliced chili
instructions:
1. Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and turmeric in stock for 15 minutes. Strain.
2. Add coconut milk to the strained stock. Bring to a gentle simmer.
3. Add flaked fish. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
4. Simmer 5 minutes — don't boil or the coconut milk will curdle.
5. Divide cooked bún into bowls. Ladle coconut broth and fish over.
6. Serve with all the accompaniments piled on the side.
tips: The broth should be gently simmered, never boiled, once the coconut milk is added. A rolling boil will break the emulsion and make it grainy.

name: Cơm Niêu
description: Clay pot rice — jasmine rice cooked in a small clay pot over charcoal until the bottom layer forms a thin, crispy golden crust (cơm cháy). The crust is the prize: scraped from the bottom and eaten with the rice, or soaked with broth and eaten as a separate crunchy snack.
tagline: Clay pot rice with a crispy bottom — worth the wait
ingredients:
- 300g jasmine rice, rinsed
- 350ml water
- 1 tsp neutral oil
- Pinch of salt
- Serve with: any Vietnamese braised dish, pickled vegetables, fish sauce
instructions:
1. Combine rice, water, oil, and salt in a small clay pot or heavy-bottomed pot.
2. Bring to a boil uncovered, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly.
3. Cook 20 minutes on very low heat.
4. Increase heat to medium for the final 5 minutes — this crisps the bottom layer.
5. Turn off heat. Let rest 10 minutes with lid on.
6. Serve from the pot: fluffy rice first, then scrape up the golden crust.
tips: The crust forms from the direct heat contact. Too low and no crust forms; too high and it burns. The 5-minute medium-heat blast at the end is the technique.

name: Chè Sương Sáo
description: Grass jelly drink — black, slightly bitter grass jelly cubed and served in a tall glass of sweetened coconut milk over ice. Cooling, deeply refreshing, and one of the simplest Vietnamese dessert drinks. The grass jelly wobbles; the coconut milk is cold and sweet; the ice melts just slowly enough.
tagline: Grass jelly drink, dark and mysteriously refreshing
ingredients:
- 200g grass jelly (sương sáo), store-bought, cut into small cubes
- 300ml coconut milk
- 2 tbsp sugar or simple syrup, to taste
- Pinch of salt
- Ice
instructions:
1. Mix coconut milk with sugar and a pinch of salt. Stir until sugar dissolves. Chill.
2. Cut grass jelly into 1cm cubes.
3. Fill tall glasses with ice.
4. Add grass jelly cubes.
5. Pour sweetened coconut milk over.
6. Stir gently and drink immediately.
tips: The salted coconut milk makes the sweetness more complex and rounds out the slight bitterness of the grass jelly. Don't skip the salt.
